item.title: Dell XPS 16 (2024) review
item.itunes:title: Dell XPS 16 (2024) review
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Key Takeaways

  • Dell's new XPS lineup brings a radical design change, from square keys to a capacitive touchpad and haptic buttons.
  • The XPS 16 stands out with its 16-inch 4K OLED display, top-notch performance, and shockingly good battery life.
  • While the XPS 16 may be the best 16-inch laptop available, getting used to the new keyboard and touchpad design may take time.

You'll rarely see companies get experimental with their flagship laptops, particularly their larger models. But Dell did exactly that when it revealed the new XPS 13, XPS 14, and XPS 16 at CES. They all adopt the design from the XPS 13 Plus, completely abandoning the traditional form factor we're all familiar with.

Dell's XPS laptops always sit on our best laptops list, and they earn the spot. And while that's still true, things are different now. The keys aren't islanded anymore, and the function row is capacitive. The touchpad is haptic and borderless.

There's one other thing that's different; the screen is 16 inches. The XPS 14 and XPS 16 take the place of the XPS 15 and XPS 17. Practically speaking, I think the Dell XPS 16 is the one that replaces both of them, while the XPS 13 and XPS 14 exist in this smaller-screen category where they really shouldn't exist side-by-side.

Related
Dell XPS 17 (2023) review: The best 17-inch laptop on the planet
Once again, Dell built a winner with the XPS 17.

Dell sent us the XPS 16 for review. It did not have any input on the contents of this article.

Dell XPS 16
The most unique
8/ 10

The Dell XPS 16 is a brand-new 16-inch laptop that's armed with the latest Intel Core Ultra processors and RTX 40-series GPUs. Compact and sleek, this premium device can also be configured to 64GB of LPDDR5x memory and a 4K+ OLED screen.  

Pros
  • A much-needed webcam improvement
  • Display quality is top notch as always
  • Performance is what you're looking for
  • Battery life is shockingly good
Cons
  • Square keys aren't for everyone
  • Borderless touchpad is imperfect
$1899 at Dell

Dell XPS 16 (2024) specs, pricing, and availability

The Dell XPS 16 (2024) became available on February 20, 2024, so you can buy one right now. It starts at $1,899, and that'll get you a Core Ultra 7 155H, 16GB RAM, a 512GB SSD, integrated Arc graphics, and an FHD display.

The model that Dell sent me for review comes in at $3,399, and that includes a Core Ultra 7 155H, 32GB RAM, a 1TB SSD, a 60W RTX 4070, and a 4K OLED display.

Specifications
CPU
Up to Intel Core Ultra 9 185H
GPU
Intel Arc (integrated), up to Nvidia RTX 4070 Laptop GPU
RAM
Up to 64GB LPDDR5x-7467MHz
Storage
Up to 4TB M.2 PCIe 4.0 NVMe SSD
Battery
99.5Wh
Display (Size, Resolution)
16.3 inches, 16:10 aspect ratio, Dolby Vision, 1920x1200 (FHD) with 100% sRGB or 3840x2400 (UHD+) with 100% DCI-P3
Camera
FHD (1080p) with IR
Speakers
Quad speakers (10W total), Waves MaxxAudio Pro, Waves Nx 3D, Dolby Atmos
Colors
Platinum, Graphite
Ports
Three Thunderbolt 4, UHS-II microSD card reader, 3.5mm audio jack
Network
Intel Killer Wi-Fi 7, Bluetooth 5.4
Dimensions
14.1x9.4x0.74 inches (358.1x240x18.7mm)
Weight
From 4.7 pounds (2.13kg)
Power
130W AC adapter
Operating System
Windows 11 Home or Pro
Price
From $1,899

Design

It's not your traditional XPS

In 2020, the Dell XPS 15 got its first major redesign in five years, and the XPS 17 was released for the first time in over a decade. With the total makeover that we saw at CES this year, the XPS designs are once again aligned across the board.

From the outside, things don't look that different. Dell sent me the Platinum model, although there's also Graphite if you're a fan of the darker colors. It's similar to what we saw from the XPS 13 Plus over the last couple of years, just bigger.

Related
Dell XPS 13 Plus (2023) review: It still feels like the future
Dell's new XPS 13 Plus is an incremental upgrade to an already modern take on a laptop

The sides of the XPS 15 have a matte metal finish, and the only USB ports are Type-C. All three of them are Thunderbolt 4, which is actually an improvement over the XPS 15, which had two Thunderbolt ports and a USB 3.2 port. On the right side, you'll also find a microSD card slot and a 3.5mm audio jack.

The XPS 16 design is business on the outside, party on the inside.

One thing I love about the XPS 16's design is that while the product is a radical redesign, it's sophisticated on the outside. The product is stylish, while it's not screaming for attention. Of course, if you look closely at the inside of it, it absolutely is.

Display

16.3 inches of 4K OLED

The Dell XPS 16 comes with a 16.3-inch 3840x2400 OLED display, although there's an option for a 1920x1200 panel as well. As always, I recommend shelling out for the OLED. In fact, even before Dell used OLED in XPS, there was a notable difference in quality between the 4K panel and the FHD panel.

According to my testing, the XPS 16 display supports 100% sRGB, 87% NTSC, 89% Adobe RGB, and 100% P3, which is fine. That's actually among the lower range of what I'd expect from a premium OLED display, so I was expecting a bit better.

Brightness came in at 408.5 nits, which is a bit more than the promised 400, and contrast is 13940:1 thanks to the black level of 0.03. Note that the black level didn't change when the brightness did, obviously because of the OLED technology.

Above the display is a 1080p webcam. Yes, Dell has finally started to catch up with the industry by offering Full HD. For years, it prioritized narrow bezels over webcam quality, but in the age of working from home, that's really not an option anymore.

Dell has finally stepped up its webcam game.

Thanks to the NPU in Intel's Core Ultra processors, it supports Windows Studio Effects, so you can blur your background on-device. Why wouldn't you continue to just use the background blur feature in Google Meet, Zoom, or Teams? I haven't gotten a concrete answer from anyone. Honestly, the NPU stuff is just starting to get useful, and those early implementations of it feel more like a proof of concept to show what could be done. However, if you're planning to keep your laptop for a few years, I do recommend getting one with an NPU, because like I said, these new features are starting to emerge.

Keyboard

You won't find another 16-inch laptop like this

This is where the radical part of the redesign comes into play. First of all, the keys aren't islanded, meaning there's no space between the keys. I don't think you'll find another 16-inch laptop like this. When companies aim to try something different, they usually do it with smaller 13- or 14-inch models.

Also, it has a haptic touchpad with no border. Obviously, the XPS 13 Plus has had both of these for a couple of years, and we've also seen it from the LG Gram Style, although LG had a solution where the sides lit up while you'd use it.

This all takes some getting used to, and I can't tell you if you'll like it. If you think you can get behind this design, go out and buy it. Almost every retailer has at least a 14-day return policy. The touchpad is particularly hard to get used to, because 98% of the time, it's just natural muscle memory, but that other 2% of the time is so frustrating. All you have to do is drag something off of the touch-sensitive area.

Finally, it has capacitive buttons instead of a function key row, which I absolutely love. If you press the Fn key, they change from settings to F-keys. It's a fun and futuristic-feeling feature.

And yes, it has the brand-new Copilot key, which you can either love or not use. I think that for most people, it's replacing a button we didn't use anyway, and if Copilot gets truly useful, we'll be happy it's there.

Performance

The performance and battery life remind me of a MacBook Pro

Performance from the Dell XPS 16 is about what you'd expect from something with its specs. After all, for regular productivity-related tasks, it has a newer CPU but the game GPU as last year's XPS 17, and Intel Core Ultra is actually a bit weaker on single-threaded performance than 13th-gen was.

Dell XPS 16 (2024)

There are some key overall differences. Intel Core Ultra is the biggest change to the company's mobile processors in decades, to the point where it's not called 14th-gen. It's Core Ultra (Series 1). The chips come with a neural processing unit (NPU) to handle on-device AI tasks like background blur and gaze correction, and a range of third-party apps are starting to take advantage of it as well.

The Dell XPS 16 is the first real MacBook Pro competitor I've seen from Windows.

Personally, I think that the Dell XPS 16 is the perfect laptop for photo and video editing. I used to call the XPS 15 the perfect combination of power and portability, but the XPS 16 adds another dimension: battery life. Historically, if you wanted a Windows laptop that had the power to edit video, you had to carry a charger everywhere you went because you were only looking at a couple hours of battery life. That changes here.

And if you're a fan of Windows, this is a big win. Apple has been praised by reviewers for combining power and battery life by switching to its own custom Arm processors. While I've seen many companies, such as Microsoft and HP, throw their products against the MacBook Pro (with the Surface Laptop Studio 2 and Dragonfly Pro, respectively), Dell actually pulls it off.

Related
HP Dragonfly Pro review: Trying to compete with Apple
HP and AMD say that the Dragonfly Pro laptop is made to compete with Apple's MacBook Pro, but did it succeed?

That battery life, from my testing, comes in at an average of around eight hours, or maybe a tiny bit less. As usual, I kept the screen on medium brightness, the power slider on balanced, and worked normally including writing in Chrome, and using other apps like Photoshop, OneNote, Excel, and some other productivity apps.

More specifically, my battery life readings were 413, 471, 515, and 550 minutes, so between just under seven hours to just over nine hours. Just ridiculous. Even under heavier loads, it just doesn't seem to cripple the battery. I'm comfortable taking this places without having a charger, and that's some high praise.

Dell XPS 16 Core Ultra 7 155H, RTX 4070 (60W)

Dell XPS 17 (2023) Core i7-13700H, RTX 4070 (60W)

Surface Laptop Studio 2 Core i7-13700H, RTX 4060 (80W)

PCMark 10 (AC / battery)

6,830 / 6,409

7,557 / 6,839

7,190 / 6,841

Geekbench 6 (single / multi)

2,424 / 13,814

2,628 / 13,993

2,549 / 13,126

Cinebench 2024 (single / multi / GPU)

104 / 983 / 10,393

109 / 781 / 9,897

Cinebench R23 (single / multi)

1,742 / 17,581

1,889 / 13,583

1,814 / 12,120

Crossmark (overall)

1,900

1,681

1,805

3DMark: Time Spy (regular / Extreme)

9,187 / 4,036

8,793 / 4,182

8,886 / 4,345

VRMark (Orange / Cyan / Blue)

12,432 / 9,671 / 2,701

11,182 / 2,752 / 2,681

9,386 / 5,504 / 2,572

The one thing that the MacBook Pro has going for it is that performance still takes a dip on the XPS 16 when it's not connected to AC power. It's not as severe as it tends to be with AMD processors, but with Arm chips, this seems to be a non-issue.

Should you buy the Dell XPS 16 (2024)?

You should buy the Dell XPS 16 (2024) if:

  • You've looked at a Dell XPS 13 Plus and thought it could be for you if it had a bigger screen and more power
  • You need power on the go
  • You edit photos and videos regularly
  • You're a casual gamer with a creative work flow

You should NOT buy the Dell XPS 16 (2024) if:

  • You prefer islanded keys
  • You're a very heavy touchpad user

The bottom line is that the Dell XPS 16 is probably the best 16-inch laptop that you can buy, but it takes some time to get used to the keyboard and the touchpad. To be completely honest, no review is going to tell you how quickly or easily you'll acclimate to it. All you can do is buy it, and return it if it doesn't work out for you.

I have to say though, the performance and battery life combination is something that I haven't seen before outside of Apple's MacBook Pro lineup. Historically, if you had a laptop that was powerful enough to edit videos and took it on the go, you had to bring a charger. Those days seem to be over.

Dell XPS 16

The Dell XPS 16 is a brand-new 16-inch laptop that's armed with the latest Intel Core Ultra processors and RTX 40-series GPUs. Compact and sleek, this premium device can also be configured to 64GB of LPDDR5x memory and a 4K+ OLED screen.  

$1899 at Dell

Power
130W AC adapter
Operating System
Windows 11 Home or Pro
Price
From $1,899

Design

It's not your traditional XPS

In 2020, the Dell XPS 15 got its first major redesign in five years, and the XPS 17 was released for the first time in over a decade. With the total makeover that we saw at CES this year, the XPS designs are once again aligned across the board.

From the outside, things don't look that different. Dell sent me the Platinum model, although there's also Graphite if you're a fan of the darker colors. It's similar to what we saw from the XPS 13 Plus over the last couple of years, just bigger.

Related
Dell XPS 13 Plus (2023) review: It still feels like the future
Dell's new XPS 13 Plus is an incremental upgrade to an already modern take on a laptop

The sides of the XPS 15 have a matte metal finish, and the only USB ports are Type-C. All three of them are Thunderbolt 4, which is actually an improvement over the XPS 15, which had two Thunderbolt ports and a USB 3.2 port. On the right side, you'll also find a microSD card slot and a 3.5mm audio jack.

The XPS 16 design is business on the outside, party on the inside.

One thing I love about the XPS 16's design is that while the product is a radical redesign, it's sophisticated on the outside. The product is stylish, while it's not screaming for attention. Of course, if you look closely at the inside of it, it absolutely is.

Display

16.3 inches of 4K OLED

The Dell XPS 16 comes with a 16.3-inch 3840x2400 OLED display, although there's an option for a 1920x1200 panel as well. As always, I recommend shelling out for the OLED. In fact, even before Dell used OLED in XPS, there was a notable difference in quality between the 4K panel and the FHD panel.

According to my testing, the XPS 16 display supports 100% sRGB, 87% NTSC, 89% Adobe RGB, and 100% P3, which is fine. That's actually among the lower range of what I'd expect from a premium OLED display, so I was expecting a bit better.

Brightness came in at 408.5 nits, which is a bit more than the promised 400, and contrast is 13940:1 thanks to the black level of 0.03. Note that the black level didn't change when the brightness did, obviously because of the OLED technology.

Above the display is a 1080p webcam. Yes, Dell has finally started to catch up with the industry by offering Full HD. For years, it prioritized narrow bezels over webcam quality, but in the age of working from home, that's really not an option anymore.

Dell has finally stepped up its webcam game.

Thanks to the NPU in Intel's Core Ultra processors, it supports Windows Studio Effects, so you can blur your background on-device. Why wouldn't you continue to just use the background blur feature in Google Meet, Zoom, or Teams? I haven't gotten a concrete answer from anyone. Honestly, the NPU stuff is just starting to get useful, and those early implementations of it feel more like a proof of concept to show what could be done. However, if you're planning to keep your laptop for a few years, I do recommend getting one with an NPU, because like I said, these new features are starting to emerge.

Keyboard

You won't find another 16-inch laptop like this

This is where the radical part of the redesign comes into play. First of all, the keys aren't islanded, meaning there's no space between the keys. I don't think you'll find another 16-inch laptop like this. When companies aim to try something different, they usually do it with smaller 13- or 14-inch models.

Also, it has a haptic touchpad with no border. Obviously, the XPS 13 Plus has had both of these for a couple of years, and we've also seen it from the LG Gram Style, although LG had a solution where the sides lit up while you'd use it.

This all takes some getting used to, and I can't tell you if you'll like it. If you think you can get behind this design, go out and buy it. Almost every retailer has at least a 14-day return policy. The touchpad is particularly hard to get used to, because 98% of the time, it's just natural muscle memory, but that other 2% of the time is so frustrating. All you have to do is drag something off of the touch-sensitive area.

Finally, it has capacitive buttons instead of a function key row, which I absolutely love. If you press the Fn key, they change from settings to F-keys. It's a fun and futuristic-feeling feature.

And yes, it has the brand-new Copilot key, which you can either love or not use. I think that for most people, it's replacing a button we didn't use anyway, and if Copilot gets truly useful, we'll be happy it's there.

Performance

The performance and battery life remind me of a MacBook Pro

Performance from the Dell XPS 16 is about what you'd expect from something with its specs. After all, for regular productivity-related tasks, it has a newer CPU but the game GPU as last year's XPS 17, and Intel Core Ultra is actually a bit weaker on single-threaded performance than 13th-gen was.

Dell XPS 16 (2024)

There are some key overall differences. Intel Core Ultra is the biggest change to the company's mobile processors in decades, to the point where it's not called 14th-gen. It's Core Ultra (Series 1). The chips come with a neural processing unit (NPU) to handle on-device AI tasks like background blur and gaze correction, and a range of third-party apps are starting to take advantage of it as well.

The Dell XPS 16 is the first real MacBook Pro competitor I've seen from Windows.

Personally, I think that the Dell XPS 16 is the perfect laptop for photo and video editing. I used to call the XPS 15 the perfect combination of power and portability, but the XPS 16 adds another dimension: battery life. Historically, if you wanted a Windows laptop that had the power to edit video, you had to carry a charger everywhere you went because you were only looking at a couple hours of battery life. That changes here.

And if you're a fan of Windows, this is a big win. Apple has been praised by reviewers for combining power and battery life by switching to its own custom Arm processors. While I've seen many companies, such as Microsoft and HP, throw their products against the MacBook Pro (with the Surface Laptop Studio 2 and Dragonfly Pro, respectively), Dell actually pulls it off.

Related
HP Dragonfly Pro review: Trying to compete with Apple
HP and AMD say that the Dragonfly Pro laptop is made to compete with Apple's MacBook Pro, but did it succeed?

That battery life, from my testing, comes in at an average of around eight hours, or maybe a tiny bit less. As usual, I kept the screen on medium brightness, the power slider on balanced, and worked normally including writing in Chrome, and using other apps like Photoshop, OneNote, Excel, and some other productivity apps.

More specifically, my battery life readings were 413, 471, 515, and 550 minutes, so between just under seven hours to just over nine hours. Just ridiculous. Even under heavier loads, it just doesn't seem to cripple the battery. I'm comfortable taking this places without having a charger, and that's some high praise.

Dell XPS 16 Core Ultra 7 155H, RTX 4070 (60W)

Dell XPS 17 (2023) Core i7-13700H, RTX 4070 (60W)

Surface Laptop Studio 2 Core i7-13700H, RTX 4060 (80W)

PCMark 10 (AC / battery)

6,830 / 6,409

7,557 / 6,839

7,190 / 6,841

Geekbench 6 (single / multi)

2,424 / 13,814

2,628 / 13,993

2,549 / 13,126

Cinebench 2024 (single / multi / GPU)

104 / 983 / 10,393

109 / 781 / 9,897

Cinebench R23 (single / multi)

1,742 / 17,581

1,889 / 13,583

1,814 / 12,120

Crossmark (overall)

1,900

1,681

1,805

3DMark: Time Spy (regular / Extreme)

9,187 / 4,036

8,793 / 4,182

8,886 / 4,345

VRMark (Orange / Cyan / Blue)

12,432 / 9,671 / 2,701

11,182 / 2,752 / 2,681

9,386 / 5,504 / 2,572

The one thing that the MacBook Pro has going for it is that performance still takes a dip on the XPS 16 when it's not connected to AC power. It's not as severe as it tends to be with AMD processors, but with Arm chips, this seems to be a non-issue.

Should you buy the Dell XPS 16 (2024)?

You should buy the Dell XPS 16 (2024) if:

  • You've looked at a Dell XPS 13 Plus and thought it could be for you if it had a bigger screen and more power
  • You need power on the go
  • You edit photos and videos regularly
  • You're a casual gamer with a creative work flow

You should NOT buy the Dell XPS 16 (2024) if:

  • You prefer islanded keys
  • You're a very heavy touchpad user

The bottom line is that the Dell XPS 16 is probably the best 16-inch laptop that you can buy, but it takes some time to get used to the keyboard and the touchpad. To be completely honest, no review is going to tell you how quickly or easily you'll acclimate to it. All you can do is buy it, and return it if it doesn't work out for you.

I have to say though, the performance and battery life combination is something that I haven't seen before outside of Apple's MacBook Pro lineup. Historically, if you had a laptop that was powerful enough to edit videos and took it on the go, you had to bring a charger. Those days seem to be over.

Dell XPS 16

The Dell XPS 16 is a brand-new 16-inch laptop that's armed with the latest Intel Core Ultra processors and RTX 40-series GPUs. Compact and sleek, this premium device can also be configured to 64GB of LPDDR5x memory and a 4K+ OLED screen.  

$1899 at Dell

item.title: Anker 521 Power Strip - Charging Station with 3 Outlets is now 43% off
item.itunes:title: Anker 521 Power Strip - Charging Station with 3 Outlets is now 43% off
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Anker 521 USB-C Power Strip
$23 $40 Save $17

A fantastic power strip that has plenty of ports and is great for travel. Right now, you can score this power strip for 43% off for a limited time, dropping the price to just $22.99.

$23 at Amazon

Anker's 521 power strip is one of the best available if you're looking for something that's compact and easy to travel with. The device delivers when it comes to connectivity with its six different charging ports. While it typically comes in at $39.99, it can now be had for much less if you're an Amazon Prime member, with a new discount that knocks 43% off, bringing the price down to $22.99 for a limited time.

Related
Best USB PD fast chargers for phones and laptops in 2024
Planning to buy a USB PD fast charger for your phone or laptop? We've selected the best USB PD chargers on the market.

What's great about the Anker 521 power strip?

When it comes to charging ports, you're going to get three AC outlets in the rear, with two USB-A and one USB-C port in the front. You get charging speeds up to 30W with the USB-C port, which is great if you're looking to charge your smartphone, tablet, or even some laptop. Of course, since this is an Anker product, you're going to get excellent reliability, with the brand's own ActiveShield technology.

In addition, Anker offers an 18-month warranty, along with a $200,000 connected equipment warranty as well. For the most part, you really can't go wrong with the Anker 521 power strip, with its versatility and compact size. Even more so now with its fantastic discount that brings it down to its lowest price yet. So grab it while you can, because it is great for travel or even good to have in your home or office.

Of course, if you're looking for other options, you can always check out some great GaN chargers, which provide more power and are great for charging larger devices like laptops.

item.title: Razer Blade 14 (2024) vs Alienware x14: Which is right for you?
item.itunes:title: Razer Blade 14 (2024) vs Alienware x14: Which is right for you?
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
• Razer Blade 14

    This sleek, powerful laptop from Razer sports an AMD Ryzen 9 CPU, up to an Nvidia GeForce RTX 4070 GPU, up to 32GB of DDR5 RAM, and an impressive QHD+ 240Hz display. If you're in the market for a meaty gaming laptop that can handle high-refresh-rate gaming on the go, this is it.

    Pros
    • 240Hz display
    • Powerful Ryzen chip
    • Great for gaming
    Cons
    • Not much storage
    $2700 at Best Buy$2200 at Razer
  • Alienware x14 R2
    $2000 $2300 Save $300

    With Intel's 13th-generation CPUs and up to an Nvidia GeForce RTX 4060 GPU, Dell's Alienware x14 R2 offers up some serious portable power. On top of a futuristic design, you're getting a QHD+ display with a 165Hz refresh rate and up to 32GB of LPDDR5 RAM, too. Most importantly, this laptop won't break the bank, either.

    Pros
    • 13th-gen Intel chip
    • Affordable
    • Attractive design
    Cons
    • Limited to RTX 4060
    $1400 at Dell$2000 at Micro Center

If you're looking for a new laptop, there are inevitably going to be lots of different machines to choose from. Razer and Alienware are popular names in the laptop space, and accordingly, Razer's Blade laptops and Alienware's x14 laptops are popular choices. However, with a new Razer Blade 14 coming out in 2024, is the Blade more worth it than last year's Alienware x14 R2?

Not to worry if you're confused. We've got the ultimate comparison between the Razer Blade 14 (2024) and the Alienware x14 R2 to help you make the best possible choice.

Razer Blade 14 (2024) vs Alienware x14: Price, availability, and specs

Both are capable, but the Razer is a tad more modern

You can pick up a Razer Blade 14 at your favorite retailer of choice, and prices kick off at $2200 for a Blade with an RTX 4060 and 16GB of RAM. Depending on your configuration, prices will, naturally, go up from there. In terms of colorways, you get the choice of either Black or Mercury.

The Alienware x14 is similarly available at a variety of retailers, and pricing starts off at $1400 for a model with an i5-13420H, an RTX 3050, and 16GB of RAM. Depending on your configuration, and how much power you need, prices can go much higher. For color, you're stuck with Lunar Silver.


  		• Razer Blade 14Alienware x14 R2
    Operating SystemWindows 11 HomeWindows 11
    CPUAMD Ryzen 9 8945HSIntel Core i5-13420H (8 cores, 12 threads, 12 MB cache, up to 4.60 GHz) or i7-13620H (10 cores, 16 threads, 24 MB, up to 4.90 GHz Turbo)
    GPUUp to Nvidia GeForce RTX 4070Up to Nvidia GeForce RTX 4060 (8 GB GDDR6)
    RAMUp to 32GB of DDR5 5600MHzUp to 32 GB LPDDR5 4800 MHz (Integrated)
    Storage1TB SSDUp to 4 TB M.2 PCIe NVMe SSD
    Battery68.1Whr80Wh
    Display (Size, Resolution)14-inch QHD+ 240Hz, 2560 x 1600, FreeSync14-inch 14'' QHD+ (2560 x 1600), 165Hz, 3ms, 300nits, DCIP3 100%
    CameraFHD IR Windows Hello webcam1080p FHD RGB+IR camera
    SpeakersStereo speakers with THX Spatial AudioTwo 2W speakers, Dolby Atmos
    ColorsBlack, MercuryLunar Silver
    Ports2 x USB 3.2 Gen 2 Type-A Ports, 2 x USB4 Type-C Ports, HDMI 2.1, 3.5mm headphone/microphone combo1x USB 3.2 Gen 2 Type-C, 1x HDMI 2.1, 1x USB 3.2 Gen 1 Type-A, 2x Thunderbolt 4, 1x MicroSD-card slot, 1x headset jack
    Weight4.05 pounds4.22 pounds
    ConnectivityQualcomm® NCM865 Wi-Fi 7, Bluetooth 5.4Intel Wi-Fi 6E AX211, 2x2, 802.11ax, MU-MIMO, Bluetooth
    Size17.99 x 228 x 310.7mm (0.70 x 8.97 x 12.23 inches)321.08 x 260.41 x 14.50mm (12.64 x 10.25 x 0.57 inches)
    Starting Price$2,199$1,400
Related
Razer Blade 14 (2023) vs Alienware x14 R2: Which portable gaming laptop is the best?
The Razer Blade 14 (2023) and the Alienware x14 R2 are fantastic gaming laptops. But which one is better for you?
Related
Best accessories for Razer Blade 14 (2023)
The Razer Blade 14 (2023) is a great gaming laptop, but these headsets, microphones, keyboards, and mice can make your laptop even better

Design and display

Similar machines with some key differences

Both of these laptops sport relatively traditional clamshell designs, but the Blade is certainly more on the traditional end. While sleek and premium, the Blade 14 is largely just a folding rectangle with some rounded edges. The Alienware x14 has a more exciting look to it with its display elevated slightly over its chassis, but in general, it's also a relatively subdued laptop.

The two of these machines look and feel like well-built computers, and while the Alienware x14 is a bit more eye-catching, both laptops will pretty easily fade into the background. If you aren't a fan of the gamer aesthetic or anything too ostentatious, you'll be right at home with either. The Alienware is wider, taller, and heavier than the Blade, while the Blade is slightly thicker. Though, the biggest difference is weight at 4.05 pounds vs 4.22 pounds.

When it comes to display, both PCs feature QHD+ 2560 x 1600 panels, while the Blade features a 240Hz refresh rate and FreeSync support and the Alienware x14 has a 165Hz refresh rate and supports G-SYNC. The Blade's display can output up to 500 nits of brightness, while the Alienware is capped at 300 nits. These displays are similar, but thanks to a higher refresh rate and better overall brightness, the Blade pulls ahead with its display.

With webcams, both laptops feature FHD IR cameras, so while you won't be shooting any movies on either, you won't have trouble popping into the occasional Zoom meeting on these computers. Altogether, the design of these laptops plays it safe, and the webcams are a match, while if you're looking for the best display, that honor goes to the Razer Blade.

Hardware and performance

More power on the Blade

Source: Dell

For raw hardware, the Razer Blade features faster RAM (5600MHz vs 4800MHz) and a more capable GPU (up to an RTX 4070 vs up to an RTX 4060). The RAM difference may not be night and day, but for gamers, you will definitely feel a difference in games going down from a 4070 to 4060. For the CPU, the Blade features a Ryzen 9 8945HS, while the Alienware x14 comes with either an i5-13420H or i7-13620H 13th-generation Intel CPU, depending on configuration.

The Ryzen chip features a higher boost clock than either Intel chip, and it has more threads than the i5. In general, benchmarks do suggest that these chips are within range of each other but that the Ryzen, on average, tends to perform slightly better. This will, of course, depend on your settings and use case, so for most, we wouldn't necessarily expect there to be a stark performance differential between the two, but your personal mileage will naturally vary.

Taken together, the Blade's faster memory, option for better GPU, and more performant CPU mean that if you're looking to maximize performance, the Blade 14 is your best bet. However, the gap in power here isn't particularly large, so unless it's very important to you as a gamer or for your workload to have the most power possible, the difference in performance may not matter to you.

Battery life

A win for the Blade

We haven't had a chance to review these laptops just yet, so we don't have our own battery life numbers; however, there are still some takeaways.

For one, Razer estimates the Blade can run for 10hrs off a single charge, while Dell estimates that the Alienware x14 can run for 5hrs on a single charge, which is half that of the Blade. Of course, manufacturer claims on battery life are usually generous, and battery life itself is incredibly variable depending on your settings and what you're doing, so take these numbers with a grain of salt.

Nonetheless, considering Dell rates the Alienware as capable of half the battery life of the Blade, we'd expect the Blade to last longer. However, keep in mind that for gaming laptops with powerful GPUs and CPUs, none of these kinds of machines will last more than a few hours when you're hitting your laptop especially hard with some high-refresh-rate gaming, so don't expect the moon.

Razer Blade 14 (2024) vs Alienware x14: Which gaming laptop is right for you?

At the end of the day, the Razer Blade 14 comes with a more capable display, the option for a more powerful GPU, a top-notch Ryzen CPU, faster RAM, and likely better battery life, making it the all-around better laptop if your main concern if figuring out which is the best machine.

It will cost you more than an Alienware x14, so if you're on a particularly tight budget, you may still prefer the Alienware, though, which is not a bad machine, either. While it makes more compromises when it comes to hardware, depending on your job and what games you'd like to play, you may be willing to make those compromises in pursuit of shaving hundreds off the price tag.

Razer Blade 14
Winner

This sleek, powerful laptop from Razer sports an AMD Ryzen 9 CPU, up to an Nvidia GeForce RTX 4070 GPU, up to 32GB of DDR5 RAM, and an impressive QHD+ 240Hz display. If you're in the market for a meaty gaming laptop that can handle high-refresh-rate gaming on the go, this is it.

$2700 at Best Buy$2200 at Razer

The Razer Blade (2024) is an impressive laptop. With up to an RTX 4070 GPU, a powerful Ryzen 9 chip, up to 32GB of RAM, and a QHD+ 240Hz display, this computer has some serious gaming chops that can handle just about any game you throw at it. Plus, it's got a sleek, premium design, too. If you have the money to spend on a Blade, you likely won't be disappointed.

Alienware x14 R2
Runner-up
$2000 $2300 Save $300

With Intel's 13th-generation CPUs and up to an Nvidia GeForce RTX 4060 GPU, Dell's Alienware x14 R2 offers up some serious portable power. On top of a futuristic design, you're getting a QHD+ display with a 165Hz refresh rate and up to 32GB of LPDDR5 RAM, too. Most importantly, this laptop won't break the bank, either.

$1400 at Dell$2000 at Micro Center

Design and display

Similar machines with some key differences

Both of these laptops sport relatively traditional clamshell designs, but the Blade is certainly more on the traditional end. While sleek and premium, the Blade 14 is largely just a folding rectangle with some rounded edges. The Alienware x14 has a more exciting look to it with its display elevated slightly over its chassis, but in general, it's also a relatively subdued laptop.

The two of these machines look and feel like well-built computers, and while the Alienware x14 is a bit more eye-catching, both laptops will pretty easily fade into the background. If you aren't a fan of the gamer aesthetic or anything too ostentatious, you'll be right at home with either. The Alienware is wider, taller, and heavier than the Blade, while the Blade is slightly thicker. Though, the biggest difference is weight at 4.05 pounds vs 4.22 pounds.

When it comes to display, both PCs feature QHD+ 2560 x 1600 panels, while the Blade features a 240Hz refresh rate and FreeSync support and the Alienware x14 has a 165Hz refresh rate and supports G-SYNC. The Blade's display can output up to 500 nits of brightness, while the Alienware is capped at 300 nits. These displays are similar, but thanks to a higher refresh rate and better overall brightness, the Blade pulls ahead with its display.

With webcams, both laptops feature FHD IR cameras, so while you won't be shooting any movies on either, you won't have trouble popping into the occasional Zoom meeting on these computers. Altogether, the design of these laptops plays it safe, and the webcams are a match, while if you're looking for the best display, that honor goes to the Razer Blade.

Hardware and performance

More power on the Blade

Source: Dell

For raw hardware, the Razer Blade features faster RAM (5600MHz vs 4800MHz) and a more capable GPU (up to an RTX 4070 vs up to an RTX 4060). The RAM difference may not be night and day, but for gamers, you will definitely feel a difference in games going down from a 4070 to 4060. For the CPU, the Blade features a Ryzen 9 8945HS, while the Alienware x14 comes with either an i5-13420H or i7-13620H 13th-generation Intel CPU, depending on configuration.

The Ryzen chip features a higher boost clock than either Intel chip, and it has more threads than the i5. In general, benchmarks do suggest that these chips are within range of each other but that the Ryzen, on average, tends to perform slightly better. This will, of course, depend on your settings and use case, so for most, we wouldn't necessarily expect there to be a stark performance differential between the two, but your personal mileage will naturally vary.

Taken together, the Blade's faster memory, option for better GPU, and more performant CPU mean that if you're looking to maximize performance, the Blade 14 is your best bet. However, the gap in power here isn't particularly large, so unless it's very important to you as a gamer or for your workload to have the most power possible, the difference in performance may not matter to you.

Battery life

A win for the Blade

We haven't had a chance to review these laptops just yet, so we don't have our own battery life numbers; however, there are still some takeaways.

For one, Razer estimates the Blade can run for 10hrs off a single charge, while Dell estimates that the Alienware x14 can run for 5hrs on a single charge, which is half that of the Blade. Of course, manufacturer claims on battery life are usually generous, and battery life itself is incredibly variable depending on your settings and what you're doing, so take these numbers with a grain of salt.

Nonetheless, considering Dell rates the Alienware as capable of half the battery life of the Blade, we'd expect the Blade to last longer. However, keep in mind that for gaming laptops with powerful GPUs and CPUs, none of these kinds of machines will last more than a few hours when you're hitting your laptop especially hard with some high-refresh-rate gaming, so don't expect the moon.

Razer Blade 14 (2024) vs Alienware x14: Which gaming laptop is right for you?

At the end of the day, the Razer Blade 14 comes with a more capable display, the option for a more powerful GPU, a top-notch Ryzen CPU, faster RAM, and likely better battery life, making it the all-around better laptop if your main concern if figuring out which is the best machine.

It will cost you more than an Alienware x14, so if you're on a particularly tight budget, you may still prefer the Alienware, though, which is not a bad machine, either. While it makes more compromises when it comes to hardware, depending on your job and what games you'd like to play, you may be willing to make those compromises in pursuit of shaving hundreds off the price tag.

Razer Blade 14
Winner

This sleek, powerful laptop from Razer sports an AMD Ryzen 9 CPU, up to an Nvidia GeForce RTX 4070 GPU, up to 32GB of DDR5 RAM, and an impressive QHD+ 240Hz display. If you're in the market for a meaty gaming laptop that can handle high-refresh-rate gaming on the go, this is it.

$2700 at Best Buy$2200 at Razer

The Razer Blade (2024) is an impressive laptop. With up to an RTX 4070 GPU, a powerful Ryzen 9 chip, up to 32GB of RAM, and a QHD+ 240Hz display, this computer has some serious gaming chops that can handle just about any game you throw at it. Plus, it's got a sleek, premium design, too. If you have the money to spend on a Blade, you likely won't be disappointed.

Alienware x14 R2
Runner-up
$2000 $2300 Save $300

With Intel's 13th-generation CPUs and up to an Nvidia GeForce RTX 4060 GPU, Dell's Alienware x14 R2 offers up some serious portable power. On top of a futuristic design, you're getting a QHD+ display with a 165Hz refresh rate and up to 32GB of LPDDR5 RAM, too. Most importantly, this laptop won't break the bank, either.

$1400 at Dell$2000 at Micro Center

item.title: 5 reasons to use AMD in your next gaming PC
item.itunes:title: 5 reasons to use AMD in your next gaming PC
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Key Takeaways

  • Enjoy top-tier gaming performance at a fraction of the cost with AMD's unmatched price-to-performance ratio.
  • Unlock your PC's full potential with AMD's smart technologies like FidelityFX and Smart Access Memory.
  • Future-proof your gaming setup with AMD's commitment to upgradability and seamless Linux support.

When it comes to purchasing or building a new gaming PC, one of the crucial decisions you face is which company's hardware you’ll use: Nvidia, Intel, or AMD. Despite being seen as an underdog in the tech sphere, AMD stands as a fierce competitor in the gaming market, challenging industry giants like Nvidia and Intel simultaneously.

Deciding to use AMD for your gaming setup goes beyond simply choosing the cheaper option; it’s about giving your PC higher performance at an accessible price point. Having consistently pushed the limits of CPU and GPU technology, AMD has firmly established itself as the preferred option for many gamers in search of top-tier performance at an affordable price.

From their Ryzen processors to their Radeon graphics cards, AMD's product lineup offers a compelling combination of power, efficiency, and value, distinguishing it in today's competitive gaming landscape.

Price to performance

Gaming at a discount

Source: AMD

AMD boasts the most significant price-to-performance ratio among CPU and GPU manufacturers. While AMD-based GPUs and CPUs generally match Nvidia and Intel counterparts in performance, they often launch a few hundred dollars less in price. This affordability allows AMD users to enjoy excellent in-game performance without straining their budgets.

AMD's commitment to delivering exceptional value per performance extends beyond the initial purchase. Regular driver updates and optimization efforts ensure that AMD CPUs and GPUs maintain their performance edge over time, keeping your gaming rig competitive even as new titles and technologies emerge.

AMD's aggressive pricing strategy benefits consumers and fosters healthy market competition, driving innovation and lowering prices across the board. By opting to use AMD for your gaming PC, you're not just getting a great deal; you're supporting a company that prioritizes affordability and accessibility for gamers of all budgets.

AMD smart technologies

Extra features that increase frame rate

AMD Smart Technology takes gaming performance to the next level by using the power of AMD Ryzen CPUs in combination with AMD Radeon graphics. This combination enables a range of innovative features designed to enhance the gaming experience. While not all of these features are exclusive to AMD they will still boost your in-game performance.

One standout feature is FidelityFX Super Resolution, AMD’s version of DLSS, a technology that intelligently manages the rendering and upscaling processes in-game. With this feature enabled, you can lower your resolution in-game and your AMD PC will upscale the resolution and sharpen the image. Enabling this feature in combination with AMD’s new Fluid Motion Frames feature can increase your FPS by up to 97%.

Another noteworthy feature is AMD’s Smart Access Memory (SAM) technology, which plays a pivotal role in boosting FPS without introducing latency. By providing AMD Ryzen processors with immediate and full access to all of your AMD Radeon graphics memory, this technology facilitates faster data transfers between the CPU and GPU. This technology is based on resizable BAR, which is not unique to AMD, but the increase in FPS and major performance boost it provides offer AMD users a superior gaming experience.

Lastly, you will have access to Radeon Anti-Lag and Anti-Lag+. This feature helps reduce lag in a game when you use a GPU that is slower than your CPU. AMD’s Anti-Lag will limit the amount of frames pushed by your CPU to match your GPU so that the CPU isn't overloaded with unnecessary work. This is a useful feature if you play shooter or action games that require high FPS.

AMD also offers a slew of optimization settings like Radeon Boost and Radeon Chill, which can increase or lower the power consumption of your Radeon graphics cards when you game, or AMD’s new Hypr-RX label which will automatically turn on the best gaming features so that you don't have to sit around and tweak settings individually.

All these features combined allow AMD users to optimize their system to play games with better FPS and smoother performance without pushing their PC to its limits.

Easy overclocking

Get the most out of your hardware without the fuss

While most companies allow overclocking of hardware, only some provide easily accessible options. AMD offers all overclocking options for GPUs and other components through the AMD Software and Ryzen Master packages.

AMD Software Adrenalin allows users to adjust their clock frequency, voltage, VRAM tuning, and fan and power supply power levels for their GPU. For CPU overclocking, you can use AMD's Ryzen Master application. These applications give AMD users the freedom to fine-tune settings on their gaming PCs without constantly rebooting their system, giving users a much better experience when overclocking their hardware.

AMD's approach to overclocking emphasizes user control and safety, with built-in safeguards and monitoring tools to prevent hardware damage and ensure system stability even under extreme settings. This level of flexibility and responsiveness makes AMD an appealing choice for overclocking enthusiasts.

AMD's commitment to overclocking extends beyond software tools, with many CPUs and GPUs designed with overclocking in mind. With unlocked multipliers and robust cooling solutions, AMD hardware provides the headroom and reliability needed to push your system to its limits without compromising the stability or longevity of the hardware.

Future-proofing

Avoiding obsolescence

Source: AMD

The ability to upgrade your hardware over time is crucial when considering your next gaming PC build. AMD offers a compelling upgrade advantage compared to Intel, thanks to its commitment to socket compatibility and longevity.

Unlike Intel, which introduces new CPU architectures every 2-3 years accompanied by changes in socket design, AMD has maintained socket compatibility across multiple generations of its Ryzen processors, generally only changing socket size on average every 5 years. This means that users investing in an AMD platform today can expect their motherboard to support future Ryzen CPU releases, eliminating the need for a complete motherboard overhaul with each CPU upgrade.

AMD's AM4 socket, in use since the first-generation Ryzen processors, has provided users with a stable and consistent platform for several years. Even with the transition to AM5 architecture in 2022, the company has given its word to still support the new socket until 2026. This type of socket upgrade path not only simplifies the upgrade process for users but also allows users to future-proof their systems without worrying about compatibility issues or obsolescence in the future.

AMD's focus on upgradability provides gamers with the flexibility and peace of mind to evolve their systems over time without significant cost or complexity. By choosing AMD for your gaming PC, you're investing in a platform that prioritizes longevity and adaptability, empowering you to stay at the forefront of gaming without breaking the bank.

Linux Support

Open-source and third-party software compatibility

Linux has become a popular operating system for developers and programmers due to its open-source software and dedicated development tools. While Linux can be used with Intel CPUs and Nvidia GPUs, compatibility issues often lead to crashes and graphical artifacts during gaming and regular operations.

AMD offers the possibility of using a Linux operating system with minimal compatibility issues and no reduction in gaming performance. AMD also actively collaborates with the Linux communities to ensure their drivers and hardware are well-supported, providing regular updates and patches to improve compatibility and address issues. This dedication to Linux compatibility makes AMD hardware an attractive choice for gamers and developers who prefer the flexibility and security of the Linux operating system.

AMD's commitment to Linux support extends beyond basic functionality, with features like AMDGPU and ROCm providing advanced graphics and compute capabilities tailored specifically for the Linux operating system. This means that if you're a casual gamer or a hardcore enthusiast, AMD's enhanced Linux support will ensure a smooth gaming experience without compatibility headaches.

Final thoughts

Choosing AMD for your next gaming PC offers a multitude of benefits that enhance your gaming experience. From superior price-to-performance ratios and integrated smart technologies to overclocking capabilities, upgradability, and robust Linux support, AMD's offerings cater to gamers of all budgets and preferences.

By embracing AMD, you're not only getting exceptional hardware but also supporting a company dedicated to innovation, affordability, and user satisfaction.

  • Future-proof your gaming setup with AMD's commitment to upgradability and seamless Linux support.

When it comes to purchasing or building a new gaming PC, one of the crucial decisions you face is which company's hardware you’ll use: Nvidia, Intel, or AMD. Despite being seen as an underdog in the tech sphere, AMD stands as a fierce competitor in the gaming market, challenging industry giants like Nvidia and Intel simultaneously.

Deciding to use AMD for your gaming setup goes beyond simply choosing the cheaper option; it’s about giving your PC higher performance at an accessible price point. Having consistently pushed the limits of CPU and GPU technology, AMD has firmly established itself as the preferred option for many gamers in search of top-tier performance at an affordable price.

From their Ryzen processors to their Radeon graphics cards, AMD's product lineup offers a compelling combination of power, efficiency, and value, distinguishing it in today's competitive gaming landscape.

Price to performance

Gaming at a discount

Source: AMD

AMD boasts the most significant price-to-performance ratio among CPU and GPU manufacturers. While AMD-based GPUs and CPUs generally match Nvidia and Intel counterparts in performance, they often launch a few hundred dollars less in price. This affordability allows AMD users to enjoy excellent in-game performance without straining their budgets.

AMD's commitment to delivering exceptional value per performance extends beyond the initial purchase. Regular driver updates and optimization efforts ensure that AMD CPUs and GPUs maintain their performance edge over time, keeping your gaming rig competitive even as new titles and technologies emerge.

AMD's aggressive pricing strategy benefits consumers and fosters healthy market competition, driving innovation and lowering prices across the board. By opting to use AMD for your gaming PC, you're not just getting a great deal; you're supporting a company that prioritizes affordability and accessibility for gamers of all budgets.

AMD smart technologies

Extra features that increase frame rate

AMD Smart Technology takes gaming performance to the next level by using the power of AMD Ryzen CPUs in combination with AMD Radeon graphics. This combination enables a range of innovative features designed to enhance the gaming experience. While not all of these features are exclusive to AMD they will still boost your in-game performance.

One standout feature is FidelityFX Super Resolution, AMD’s version of DLSS, a technology that intelligently manages the rendering and upscaling processes in-game. With this feature enabled, you can lower your resolution in-game and your AMD PC will upscale the resolution and sharpen the image. Enabling this feature in combination with AMD’s new Fluid Motion Frames feature can increase your FPS by up to 97%.

Another noteworthy feature is AMD’s Smart Access Memory (SAM) technology, which plays a pivotal role in boosting FPS without introducing latency. By providing AMD Ryzen processors with immediate and full access to all of your AMD Radeon graphics memory, this technology facilitates faster data transfers between the CPU and GPU. This technology is based on resizable BAR, which is not unique to AMD, but the increase in FPS and major performance boost it provides offer AMD users a superior gaming experience.

Lastly, you will have access to Radeon Anti-Lag and Anti-Lag+. This feature helps reduce lag in a game when you use a GPU that is slower than your CPU. AMD’s Anti-Lag will limit the amount of frames pushed by your CPU to match your GPU so that the CPU isn't overloaded with unnecessary work. This is a useful feature if you play shooter or action games that require high FPS.

AMD also offers a slew of optimization settings like Radeon Boost and Radeon Chill, which can increase or lower the power consumption of your Radeon graphics cards when you game, or AMD’s new Hypr-RX label which will automatically turn on the best gaming features so that you don't have to sit around and tweak settings individually.

All these features combined allow AMD users to optimize their system to play games with better FPS and smoother performance without pushing their PC to its limits.

Easy overclocking

Get the most out of your hardware without the fuss

While most companies allow overclocking of hardware, only some provide easily accessible options. AMD offers all overclocking options for GPUs and other components through the AMD Software and Ryzen Master packages.

AMD Software Adrenalin allows users to adjust their clock frequency, voltage, VRAM tuning, and fan and power supply power levels for their GPU. For CPU overclocking, you can use AMD's Ryzen Master application. These applications give AMD users the freedom to fine-tune settings on their gaming PCs without constantly rebooting their system, giving users a much better experience when overclocking their hardware.

AMD's approach to overclocking emphasizes user control and safety, with built-in safeguards and monitoring tools to prevent hardware damage and ensure system stability even under extreme settings. This level of flexibility and responsiveness makes AMD an appealing choice for overclocking enthusiasts.

AMD's commitment to overclocking extends beyond software tools, with many CPUs and GPUs designed with overclocking in mind. With unlocked multipliers and robust cooling solutions, AMD hardware provides the headroom and reliability needed to push your system to its limits without compromising the stability or longevity of the hardware.

Future-proofing

Avoiding obsolescence

Source: AMD

The ability to upgrade your hardware over time is crucial when considering your next gaming PC build. AMD offers a compelling upgrade advantage compared to Intel, thanks to its commitment to socket compatibility and longevity.

Unlike Intel, which introduces new CPU architectures every 2-3 years accompanied by changes in socket design, AMD has maintained socket compatibility across multiple generations of its Ryzen processors, generally only changing socket size on average every 5 years. This means that users investing in an AMD platform today can expect their motherboard to support future Ryzen CPU releases, eliminating the need for a complete motherboard overhaul with each CPU upgrade.

AMD's AM4 socket, in use since the first-generation Ryzen processors, has provided users with a stable and consistent platform for several years. Even with the transition to AM5 architecture in 2022, the company has given its word to still support the new socket until 2026. This type of socket upgrade path not only simplifies the upgrade process for users but also allows users to future-proof their systems without worrying about compatibility issues or obsolescence in the future.

AMD's focus on upgradability provides gamers with the flexibility and peace of mind to evolve their systems over time without significant cost or complexity. By choosing AMD for your gaming PC, you're investing in a platform that prioritizes longevity and adaptability, empowering you to stay at the forefront of gaming without breaking the bank.

Linux Support

Open-source and third-party software compatibility

Linux has become a popular operating system for developers and programmers due to its open-source software and dedicated development tools. While Linux can be used with Intel CPUs and Nvidia GPUs, compatibility issues often lead to crashes and graphical artifacts during gaming and regular operations.

AMD offers the possibility of using a Linux operating system with minimal compatibility issues and no reduction in gaming performance. AMD also actively collaborates with the Linux communities to ensure their drivers and hardware are well-supported, providing regular updates and patches to improve compatibility and address issues. This dedication to Linux compatibility makes AMD hardware an attractive choice for gamers and developers who prefer the flexibility and security of the Linux operating system.

AMD's commitment to Linux support extends beyond basic functionality, with features like AMDGPU and ROCm providing advanced graphics and compute capabilities tailored specifically for the Linux operating system. This means that if you're a casual gamer or a hardcore enthusiast, AMD's enhanced Linux support will ensure a smooth gaming experience without compatibility headaches.

Final thoughts

Choosing AMD for your next gaming PC offers a multitude of benefits that enhance your gaming experience. From superior price-to-performance ratios and integrated smart technologies to overclocking capabilities, upgradability, and robust Linux support, AMD's offerings cater to gamers of all budgets and preferences.

By embracing AMD, you're not only getting exceptional hardware but also supporting a company dedicated to innovation, affordability, and user satisfaction.

item.title: How to choose the perfect CPU cooler while building a new PC
item.itunes:title: How to choose the perfect CPU cooler while building a new PC
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Selecting the ideal CPU cooler is necessary not only for maintaining your processor at a safe operating temperature but also for enhancing your system's overall performance, prolonging the lifespan of your PC components, and aligning with your personal preferences and computing needs. As such, it's important to choose the right kind of cooler for your machine, whether that's air cooling, all-in-one (AIO) liquid cooling, or custom water cooling setups, and we're here to help you make that decision.

The market offers a range of solutions, which are aimed at different kinds of cooling goals: air coolers for simplicity and reliability, AIO systems for advanced cooling, and custom liquid cooling for top performance and customization. Each caters to different preferences and requirements.
Related
Best CPU coolers in 2024
Buying the best CPU cooler is just as important as buying any other core component. Here are some of the best CPU coolers on the market.

Air coolers

Top of the be quiet! Dark Rock Pro 5.

Air coolers are the go-to option for most PC builders thanks to their simplicity, ease of installation, and extremely low maintenance. An air cooler keeps your CPU cool by taking the heat from it and moving it away. The heat sink, usually made of copper or aluminum, sits on top of the integrated heat spreader (IHS) of the CPU and absorbs its heat. This heat is then carried away by heat pipes which contain a minute quantity of fluid and a wick setup. This liquid within the heat pipes has a considerably low boiling point and easily evaporates upon being heated. Once the fluid starts boiling it carries the heat further up the heat pipe which then runs through the finstack and cools down as a fan blows air over these fins.

This process repeats, keeping your CPU temperature in check. It's a cost-effective and extremely reliable setup, ensuring minimum downtime and virtually infinite longevity. The materials used for the heat sink, along with the fan's size and speed, plus how the fins are designed and arranged, all play a big part in how well these coolers do their job. Here are key factors to weigh before choosing an air cooler for your CPU:

  • Physical clearance: Air coolers come in various sizes. You should ensure the cooler you choose fits within your case and doesn't obstruct access to RAM slots, PCIe slots, or the motherboard's power connectors.
  • CPU socket compatibility: Check that the air cooler supports your CPU's socket type. While many coolers are designed to be universally compatible, it's always best to confirm.
  • TDP (Thermal Design Power): The cooler's TDP rating should meet or exceed that of your CPU to ensure adequate cooling. Overclocking increases the effective TDP, so plan accordingly.
  • Fan size and speed: Larger fans can move the same amount of air at lower speeds compared to smaller fans, resulting in quieter operation. PWM (Pulse Width Modulation) support allows the fan speed to adjust dynamically, reducing noise when full performance isn't necessary.
  • Ease of installation: Consider how easy the cooler is to install. Some air coolers come with more user-friendly mounting systems than others.

Who should consider air coolers?

  • If you are on a budget: Air coolers are your wallet's best friend. Most air coolers are very budget-friendly, and you can always find an option that checks all the boxes that you might have while considering a cooler for your next build.
  • If you want a reliable, no-fuss build: If you want something that just works and keeps working with minimal fuss, air coolers are your go-to option. A quick dust-off now and then is all the maintenance they usually need.
  • Fit and compatibility: With a variety of sizes and designs, it's easier to find an air cooler that'll fit right into your setup without any hassle. This especially comes in handy for people who might not have enough space to accommodate radiators for open or closed-loop systems.
  • Quiet operation: If you're all about keeping things quiet, many air coolers are designed just for that, even at higher RPMs, thanks to smart fan designs, improved bearings, and features that cut down on noise.

Who should avoid air coolers?

  • Overclocking aficionados: If you're looking to push your CPU to the edge of its performance, you might find air coolers a bit limiting, especially for high TDP CPUs.
  • Compact case owners: Got a small case? Some of the beefier air coolers are not the best fit.
  • Aesthetics enthusiasts: If you're aiming for a specific look inside your case, air coolers might not always match your vision. There are always those subtle options like the Be Quiet Dark Rock Pro 5 which are available, but in most cases, if you are going for a specific look, you might have a hard time tying in an air cooler with the rest of your rig.

In summary, air coolers are a great choice for many, balancing performance, cost, and ease of use. They're durable, easy to fit, and can keep your system running smoothly without too much noise. But, if you're into serious overclocking, have a small case, or are looking for a particular aesthetic, you might want to explore other options.

AIO (All-In-One) closed-loop coolers

AIO coolers represent a significant step-up in cooling efficacy, combining the simplicity of air coolers with the enhanced performance of liquid cooling in a compact, easy-to-install package. These systems work by circulating a coolant through a closed loop, transferring heat from the CPU to a radiator, where it is then expelled by one or more fans. The heart of an AIO cooler is its pump, which moves the coolant between the CPU block and the radiator. This setup allows for superior cooling performance compared to air coolers, making AIOs a popular choice among those looking to push their CPUs harder without stepping into the complexities of custom liquid cooling.

There are a few things you must consider before choosing the perfect AIO for your build.

  • Radiator Size: AIO coolers come with radiators of varying sizes, typically ranging from 120mm to 480mm. Ensure your case can accommodate the radiator size you're considering, both in terms of mounting points and clearance. The key here is to choose a radiator size that efficiently negates the total heat output of the CPU.
  • CPU socket compatibility: Verify that the AIO cooler supports your CPU socket. While most coolers are compatible with common sockets, it's always best to check, especially for less common or older socket types.
  • TDP (Thermal Design Power): The cooler you choose should be capable of handling the TDP of your CPU. Overclocking increases the TDP, so if you plan to overclock, consider coolers that offer higher cooling capacities.
  • Fan speed and type: Higher-speed fans can cool better but may generate more noise.
  • Pump noise: Some AIO pumps can be noisy at high speeds. Reviews and user feedback can be valuable resources for gauging pump noise.
  • Design and lighting: Many AIO coolers feature RGB lighting and sleek designs. Consider how a cooler's aesthetics will fit with your build's overall theme.
  • Materials and construction: The durability of the cooler is also important for its longevity. The days of leaky AIOs are mostly over, but it is still a good idea to ensure quality materials and solid construction, especially in the tubing and radiator.
  • Installation process: Some AIO coolers are easier to install than others. Consider the complexity of the mounting mechanism and the clarity of the installation instructions.

Who should consider AIO coolers?

  • Performance seekers: For users aiming to extract more performance from their CPUs, possibly through moderate overclocking, AIO coolers offer the necessary thermal headroom without the complexities and maintenance requirements of custom loops.
  • Space-conscious builders: The compact design of the CPU block in AIO systems makes them ideal for builds with limited space around the CPU socket, providing better RAM and VRM heatsink clearance compared to bulky air coolers.
  • Aesthetic and customization: AIO coolers often feature sleek designs with customizable RGB lighting, making them a favorite for builders looking to create a visually appealing setup. The radiator can also be mounted in different positions of the case, allowing for flexible airflow configurations.

Who should avoid AIO coolers?

  • Ultra-high-performance users: For those looking to push their systems to the absolute limit with extensive overclocking, the cooling performance of AIO systems, while excellent, might still fall short compared to the best-in-class custom liquid cooling setups.
  • Maintenance-conscious users: Although AIO coolers are largely maintenance-free, they can still present challenges. The risk of pump failure, although low, is a consideration, and any leakage, while extremely rare, can have serious consequences for your system.

In essence, AIO coolers strike a balance between performance, aesthetics, and ease of installation. They cater well to those looking to improve their CPU's cooling efficiency without the hassle of custom liquid cooling setups. With their blend of quiet operation, space efficiency, and visual appeal, AIO systems offer a versatile cooling solution that meets the needs of a wide range of users.

Custom liquid cooling

The basic working principle behind custom liquid cooling is similar to that of closed-loop AIO coolers. The system works by circulating the coolant between the heat-producing components of a build and cooling radiators. It allows for a tailored loop that can include not just the CPU but also GPUs and other components like the RAM and even the motherboard VRM, offering the best possible cooling performance. This level of customization extends to the choice of components, coolant colors, and even the tubing material, whether soft or hard.However, this comes at a higher cost, both financially and in terms of the time and knowledge required for installation and maintenance. Custom loops demand regular maintenance, including fluid changes and leak checks, but for enthusiasts and overclockers looking to push their systems to the limit or create a visually stunning build, there’s no better choice. However, there are a few things you should keep in mind while considering open-loop cooling.
  • Complexity and customization: Building a custom loop involves selecting individual components, such as the pump, reservoir, radiator, fittings, tubing, and coolant. This asks for a greater knowledge of the compatibility of tubes and fittings, and the know-how around bending tubes if going with a hard tube setup.
  • Choice of radiators: Correctly calculating the type and number of radiators required for your particular setup is essential to ensure the proper functioning of the loop. The rule of thumb is to consider 120 mm of radiator for each heat-producing component in your loop and another 120 mm for extra headroom. For example, let us assume you have the CPU, GPU, and RAM included in your loop. So, for the 3 components, you should have 360 mm of radiator plus an overhead of 120 mm, bringing the total up to 480mm. This can be achieved in a couple of different ways. The first method would be to employ 2 radiators of 240 mm for the ideal usage of space in a build but it might look ugly in that case. A 360mm rad in the front or the top and a 120mm rad in the rear should be sufficient while imparting a better aesthetic to your build.
  • Space and compatibility: Ensure your case has enough space to accommodate the components of a custom loop, including large radiators and the reservoir. Compatibility with your CPU and GPU is also important.
  • Maintenance: Unlike air and AIO coolers, custom loops require regular maintenance to keep them running efficiently. This includes checking for leaks, cleaning the components, and replacing the coolant periodically.
  • Cost: Custom liquid cooling is the most expensive cooling option, with the cost of components and potential maintenance adding up. However, for many enthusiasts, the investment is worth the enhanced performance and aesthetics.

Who should consider custom liquid cooling?

  • High-performance users: If you're pushing your hardware to its limits with overclocking or demanding applications, custom liquid cooling can offer the thermal headroom needed to maintain optimal performance.
  • Aesthetics and customization lovers: For those who want their PC to stand out, custom loops offer endless possibilities for customization, from the color of the coolant to the design of the tubing.
  • Tech enthusiasts with time and budget: Building and maintaining a custom loop requires time, patience, and a larger budget. If you enjoy DIY projects and are willing to invest in your setup, custom liquid cooling can be a rewarding experience.

Who might want to look elsewhere?

  • Budget-conscious builders: Due to the high cost of components and maintenance, those on a tight budget might find custom liquid cooling prohibitive.
  • Beginners or those seeking simplicity: The complexity of designing and assembling a custom loop, as well as the ongoing maintenance, might be daunting for newcomers to PC building or those looking for a simple cooling solution.

Choosing the right CPU cooler

If you prioritize affordability and have a mid-range CPU, a high-quality air cooler is advisable. It offers a balance between performance and ease of maintenance, making it ideal for budget-conscious users. Air coolers are also suitable for those who prefer a setup that is easy to maintain and relatively quiet, fitting well into most case sizes without obstructing other components.

For users with high-performance needs, such as intensive gaming, video editing, or 3D rendering, a high to mid-range AIO (All-In-One) or custom liquid cooling system is recommended. These options provide superior cooling capacity to handle high TDP CPUs, ensuring that your system maintains stability and performance under load.

Ultimately, the selection hinges on finding a cooler that aligns with your specific requirements, including the physical constraints of your PC case, the thermal demands of your CPU, and your personal preferences for noise levels and visual design.
