Ultimately, the selection hinges on finding a cooler that aligns with your specific requirements, including the physical constraints of your PC case, the thermal demands of your CPU, and your personal preferences for noise levels and visual design.
item.content:encoded:
Selecting the ideal CPU cooler is necessary not only for maintaining your processor at a safe operating temperature but also for enhancing your system's overall performance, prolonging the lifespan of your PC components, and aligning with your personal preferences and computing needs. As such, it's important to choose the right kind of cooler for your machine, whether that's air cooling, all-in-one (AIO) liquid cooling, or custom water cooling setups, and we're here to help you make that decision.

The market offers a range of solutions, which are aimed at different kinds of cooling goals: air coolers for simplicity and reliability, AIO systems for advanced cooling, and custom liquid cooling for top performance and customization. Each caters to different preferences and requirements.
Related
Best CPU coolers in 2024
Buying the best CPU cooler is just as important as buying any other core component. Here are some of the best CPU coolers on the market.

Air coolers

Top of the be quiet! Dark Rock Pro 5.

Air coolers are the go-to option for most PC builders thanks to their simplicity, ease of installation, and extremely low maintenance. An air cooler keeps your CPU cool by taking the heat from it and moving it away. The heat sink, usually made of copper or aluminum, sits on top of the integrated heat spreader (IHS) of the CPU and absorbs its heat. This heat is then carried away by heat pipes which contain a minute quantity of fluid and a wick setup. This liquid within the heat pipes has a considerably low boiling point and easily evaporates upon being heated. Once the fluid starts boiling it carries the heat further up the heat pipe which then runs through the finstack and cools down as a fan blows air over these fins.

This process repeats, keeping your CPU temperature in check. It's a cost-effective and extremely reliable setup, ensuring minimum downtime and virtually infinite longevity. The materials used for the heat sink, along with the fan's size and speed, plus how the fins are designed and arranged, all play a big part in how well these coolers do their job. Here are key factors to weigh before choosing an air cooler for your CPU:

  • Physical clearance: Air coolers come in various sizes. You should ensure the cooler you choose fits within your case and doesn't obstruct access to RAM slots, PCIe slots, or the motherboard's power connectors.
  • CPU socket compatibility: Check that the air cooler supports your CPU's socket type. While many coolers are designed to be universally compatible, it's always best to confirm.
  • TDP (Thermal Design Power): The cooler's TDP rating should meet or exceed that of your CPU to ensure adequate cooling. Overclocking increases the effective TDP, so plan accordingly.
  • Fan size and speed: Larger fans can move the same amount of air at lower speeds compared to smaller fans, resulting in quieter operation. PWM (Pulse Width Modulation) support allows the fan speed to adjust dynamically, reducing noise when full performance isn't necessary.
  • Ease of installation: Consider how easy the cooler is to install. Some air coolers come with more user-friendly mounting systems than others.

Who should consider air coolers?

  • If you are on a budget: Air coolers are your wallet's best friend. Most air coolers are very budget-friendly, and you can always find an option that checks all the boxes that you might have while considering a cooler for your next build.
  • If you want a reliable, no-fuss build: If you want something that just works and keeps working with minimal fuss, air coolers are your go-to option. A quick dust-off now and then is all the maintenance they usually need.
  • Fit and compatibility: With a variety of sizes and designs, it's easier to find an air cooler that'll fit right into your setup without any hassle. This especially comes in handy for people who might not have enough space to accommodate radiators for open or closed-loop systems.
  • Quiet operation: If you're all about keeping things quiet, many air coolers are designed just for that, even at higher RPMs, thanks to smart fan designs, improved bearings, and features that cut down on noise.

Who should avoid air coolers?

  • Overclocking aficionados: If you're looking to push your CPU to the edge of its performance, you might find air coolers a bit limiting, especially for high TDP CPUs.
  • Compact case owners: Got a small case? Some of the beefier air coolers are not the best fit.
  • Aesthetics enthusiasts: If you're aiming for a specific look inside your case, air coolers might not always match your vision. There are always those subtle options like the Be Quiet Dark Rock Pro 5 which are available, but in most cases, if you are going for a specific look, you might have a hard time tying in an air cooler with the rest of your rig.

In summary, air coolers are a great choice for many, balancing performance, cost, and ease of use. They're durable, easy to fit, and can keep your system running smoothly without too much noise. But, if you're into serious overclocking, have a small case, or are looking for a particular aesthetic, you might want to explore other options.

AIO (All-In-One) closed-loop coolers

AIO coolers represent a significant step-up in cooling efficacy, combining the simplicity of air coolers with the enhanced performance of liquid cooling in a compact, easy-to-install package. These systems work by circulating a coolant through a closed loop, transferring heat from the CPU to a radiator, where it is then expelled by one or more fans. The heart of an AIO cooler is its pump, which moves the coolant between the CPU block and the radiator. This setup allows for superior cooling performance compared to air coolers, making AIOs a popular choice among those looking to push their CPUs harder without stepping into the complexities of custom liquid cooling.

There are a few things you must consider before choosing the perfect AIO for your build.

  • Radiator Size: AIO coolers come with radiators of varying sizes, typically ranging from 120mm to 480mm. Ensure your case can accommodate the radiator size you're considering, both in terms of mounting points and clearance. The key here is to choose a radiator size that efficiently negates the total heat output of the CPU.
  • CPU socket compatibility: Verify that the AIO cooler supports your CPU socket. While most coolers are compatible with common sockets, it's always best to check, especially for less common or older socket types.
  • TDP (Thermal Design Power): The cooler you choose should be capable of handling the TDP of your CPU. Overclocking increases the TDP, so if you plan to overclock, consider coolers that offer higher cooling capacities.
  • Fan speed and type: Higher-speed fans can cool better but may generate more noise.
  • Pump noise: Some AIO pumps can be noisy at high speeds. Reviews and user feedback can be valuable resources for gauging pump noise.
  • Design and lighting: Many AIO coolers feature RGB lighting and sleek designs. Consider how a cooler's aesthetics will fit with your build's overall theme.
  • Materials and construction: The durability of the cooler is also important for its longevity. The days of leaky AIOs are mostly over, but it is still a good idea to ensure quality materials and solid construction, especially in the tubing and radiator.
  • Installation process: Some AIO coolers are easier to install than others. Consider the complexity of the mounting mechanism and the clarity of the installation instructions.

Who should consider AIO coolers?

  • Performance seekers: For users aiming to extract more performance from their CPUs, possibly through moderate overclocking, AIO coolers offer the necessary thermal headroom without the complexities and maintenance requirements of custom loops.
  • Space-conscious builders: The compact design of the CPU block in AIO systems makes them ideal for builds with limited space around the CPU socket, providing better RAM and VRM heatsink clearance compared to bulky air coolers.
  • Aesthetic and customization: AIO coolers often feature sleek designs with customizable RGB lighting, making them a favorite for builders looking to create a visually appealing setup. The radiator can also be mounted in different positions of the case, allowing for flexible airflow configurations.

Who should avoid AIO coolers?

  • Ultra-high-performance users: For those looking to push their systems to the absolute limit with extensive overclocking, the cooling performance of AIO systems, while excellent, might still fall short compared to the best-in-class custom liquid cooling setups.
  • Maintenance-conscious users: Although AIO coolers are largely maintenance-free, they can still present challenges. The risk of pump failure, although low, is a consideration, and any leakage, while extremely rare, can have serious consequences for your system.

In essence, AIO coolers strike a balance between performance, aesthetics, and ease of installation. They cater well to those looking to improve their CPU's cooling efficiency without the hassle of custom liquid cooling setups. With their blend of quiet operation, space efficiency, and visual appeal, AIO systems offer a versatile cooling solution that meets the needs of a wide range of users.

Custom liquid cooling

The basic working principle behind custom liquid cooling is similar to that of closed-loop AIO coolers. The system works by circulating the coolant between the heat-producing components of a build and cooling radiators. It allows for a tailored loop that can include not just the CPU but also GPUs and other components like the RAM and even the motherboard VRM, offering the best possible cooling performance. This level of customization extends to the choice of components, coolant colors, and even the tubing material, whether soft or hard.However, this comes at a higher cost, both financially and in terms of the time and knowledge required for installation and maintenance. Custom loops demand regular maintenance, including fluid changes and leak checks, but for enthusiasts and overclockers looking to push their systems to the limit or create a visually stunning build, there’s no better choice. However, there are a few things you should keep in mind while considering open-loop cooling.
  • Complexity and customization: Building a custom loop involves selecting individual components, such as the pump, reservoir, radiator, fittings, tubing, and coolant. This asks for a greater knowledge of the compatibility of tubes and fittings, and the know-how around bending tubes if going with a hard tube setup.
  • Choice of radiators: Correctly calculating the type and number of radiators required for your particular setup is essential to ensure the proper functioning of the loop. The rule of thumb is to consider 120 mm of radiator for each heat-producing component in your loop and another 120 mm for extra headroom. For example, let us assume you have the CPU, GPU, and RAM included in your loop. So, for the 3 components, you should have 360 mm of radiator plus an overhead of 120 mm, bringing the total up to 480mm. This can be achieved in a couple of different ways. The first method would be to employ 2 radiators of 240 mm for the ideal usage of space in a build but it might look ugly in that case. A 360mm rad in the front or the top and a 120mm rad in the rear should be sufficient while imparting a better aesthetic to your build.
  • Space and compatibility: Ensure your case has enough space to accommodate the components of a custom loop, including large radiators and the reservoir. Compatibility with your CPU and GPU is also important.
  • Maintenance: Unlike air and AIO coolers, custom loops require regular maintenance to keep them running efficiently. This includes checking for leaks, cleaning the components, and replacing the coolant periodically.
  • Cost: Custom liquid cooling is the most expensive cooling option, with the cost of components and potential maintenance adding up. However, for many enthusiasts, the investment is worth the enhanced performance and aesthetics.

Who should consider custom liquid cooling?

  • High-performance users: If you're pushing your hardware to its limits with overclocking or demanding applications, custom liquid cooling can offer the thermal headroom needed to maintain optimal performance.
  • Aesthetics and customization lovers: For those who want their PC to stand out, custom loops offer endless possibilities for customization, from the color of the coolant to the design of the tubing.
  • Tech enthusiasts with time and budget: Building and maintaining a custom loop requires time, patience, and a larger budget. If you enjoy DIY projects and are willing to invest in your setup, custom liquid cooling can be a rewarding experience.

Who might want to look elsewhere?

  • Budget-conscious builders: Due to the high cost of components and maintenance, those on a tight budget might find custom liquid cooling prohibitive.
  • Beginners or those seeking simplicity: The complexity of designing and assembling a custom loop, as well as the ongoing maintenance, might be daunting for newcomers to PC building or those looking for a simple cooling solution.

Choosing the right CPU cooler

If you prioritize affordability and have a mid-range CPU, a high-quality air cooler is advisable. It offers a balance between performance and ease of maintenance, making it ideal for budget-conscious users. Air coolers are also suitable for those who prefer a setup that is easy to maintain and relatively quiet, fitting well into most case sizes without obstructing other components.

For users with high-performance needs, such as intensive gaming, video editing, or 3D rendering, a high to mid-range AIO (All-In-One) or custom liquid cooling system is recommended. These options provide superior cooling capacity to handle high TDP CPUs, ensuring that your system maintains stability and performance under load.

Ultimately, the selection hinges on finding a cooler that aligns with your specific requirements, including the physical constraints of your PC case, the thermal demands of your CPU, and your personal preferences for noise levels and visual design.

item.title: How to lower your GPU's temperature
item.itunes:title: How to lower your GPU's temperature
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
The GPU, or graphics processing unit, is one of the core components of a PC that does a lot of heavy lifting, even when you are not playing a game. It's constantly used in varying capacities by a wide range of applications, and it's important to keep it cool at all times to ensure smooth performance. It's not particularly difficult to tame the temperature of a graphics card, but there are times when you may need to put in some additional efforts to maintain optimal performance. Nobody likes a GPU that's constantly overheating and delivering throttled performance, so read on to find some effective ways to control and lower GPU temperatures, thus safeguarding your graphics card's performance and longevity.

1 Improve case airflow

A simple and effective way to lower temperature

Optimizing the airflow within your computer case is a fundamental step in controlling GPU temperature. Modern GPUs use an array of downdraft fans that force air straight down through the card, dissipating the heat from the heatsink assembly in the process. Adequate case airflow ensures a continuous influx of cool air and efficient removal of hot air, essential for maintaining a balanced temperature.

To enhance case airflow, consider installing additional case fans in strategic locations, configuring fan orientations for optimal air movement, and establishing positive air pressure within the case to prevent dust infiltration and maintain efficient cooling. It is generally agreed upon that positive case pressure provides the ideal balance between thermal performance and dust buildup, although negative pressure can also be quite beneficial for GPU temperatures.

Related
PC airflow guide: How to set up your case fans
You'll need to set up proper airflow inside your PC case for optimal cooling. This guide will help you with that.

2 Increase fan speed

It only takes a few minutes to set a custom fan curve

Modern GPUs come equipped with built-in fans that adjust their speeds automatically based on temperature. However, you can take control of the situation by manually increasing fan speed using software tools like MSI Afterburner or the manufacturer's proprietary software. While this can effectively cool the GPU, be prepared for increased noise levels as a trade-off. Constantly running GPU fans at their maximum speed can also accelerate the wear and tear process.

Related
How to use Fan Control
Want to set up Fan Control to manage your system fans? Here's how.

3 Manage ambient temperature

Try to maintain a low ambient temperature

The ambient temperature in the room where your computer resides plays a pivotal role in GPU temperature management. A warmer room means the GPU has to work harder to dissipate heat, potentially leading to overheating. To mitigate this, ensure proper room ventilation, consider using air conditioning or fans to maintain a cooler room temperature, and avoid placing the computer in a confined space with limited air circulation.

4 Prevent dust buildup

Regular maintenance of your PC is crucial

Dust accumulation on the GPU's heatsinks and fans is a common culprit for elevated temperatures. Although it may seem rather harmless, dust is a menace to electronics, particularly those that work in a confined space. Regular maintenance is the key to preventing dust buildup. Keep your PC dust-free, and make sure that all the case filters are regularly cleaned as well. Use compressed air or specialized electronic cleaning tools to remove dust from your GPU and other components, allowing for unobstructed airflow and efficient cooling.

Related
How to clean the dust out of your PC tower
Dust buildup is bad for your system, so you should regularly clean your PC tower to keep it free of dirt and grime

5 Improve GPU placement

Leave some space for the GPU to breathe

The location of your GPU within the case can significantly impact its temperature. Ideally, install your GPU in the top PCIe slot to ensure it has ample space for airflow and heat dissipation. Moreover, it is also important to consider what orientation you want to install the graphics card in. Although the rising trend of vertical GPU mounting is quite attractive from an aesthetics standpoint, it raises the temperature of the GPU due to reduced airflow. Try to install the GPU in a horizontal position, with plenty of space between the GPU fans and the bottom of the case. Avoid obstructing the GPU with other components, cables, or storage drives, as this can impede the airflow and lead to increased temperatures.

Related
How to install a graphics card in your PC: A beginners guide
There's never a bad time to upgrade and install a new GPU. In this guide, we'll teach you how to install a new graphics card.

6 Improve cable management

Don't slack with cable management

Disorganized and tangled cables can obstruct airflow pathways and disrupt heat dissipation. To neatly organize your cables, you can use cable ties, and if necessary, remove and re-install your CPU cooler with better fans and CPU pump cable routing. Re-route the cables in a manner that ensures a clean and tidy interior, creating an unobstructed channel for airflow. This will ultimately reduce GPU temperatures. Be especially vigilant in ensuring that no cables obstruct the airflow from the intake fans to the GPU fans.

Related
The step-by-step guide for perfect PC cable management
PC cable management is an important part of the PC building process. Here's a step-by-step PC cable management guide to help you get started.

7 GPU undervolting

For those who don't mind tinkering

Undervolting is our first “invasive” step that can significantly improve your GPU temperatures, although with a bit of effort. It involves decreasing the voltage supplied to the GPU while maintaining stability in terms of performance. Undervolting is similar to overclocking, but you are essentially going the opposite way with the voltage slider and trying to achieve stability.

Undervolting can be done using software like MSI Afterburner or with manufacturer-provided tools. Essentially, it involves reducing the voltage provided to the GPU core while maintaining the stock clocks of the graphics card. Each round of undervolting should be followed by thorough stability testing if you don’t want to run into a sudden crash. To get a good measure of the overall stability, it is important to run multiple cycles on various software like Unigine Heaven, Unigine Superposition, 3DMark Suite, Furmark, and your favorite games.

By undervolting your GPU using manufacturer-provided tools or third-party applications, you can significantly reduce heat generation and power consumption. However, it's essential to find the right balance between undervolting and maintaining optimal performance, as excessive undervolting can lead to instability and a reduction in potential boost clocks.

8 Changing thermal paste

Requires a lot of effort

The thermal paste that is applied between the GPU die and its heatsink degrades over time, reducing its heat transfer efficiency. Replacing the old thermal paste with a fresh, high-quality thermal paste can substantially lower temperatures and improve cooling performance. This maintenance task can be particularly beneficial if your GPU is exhibiting signs of overheating, and is often the go-to method for enthusiasts who want to reduce GPU temperatures.

For this step, you will need to open your graphics card to separate the PCB from the heatsink assembly. Most graphics card manufacturers void your warranty for opening up the graphics card, so be mindful of this caveat. Once the two components are separated, thoroughly clean the old thermal paste application from the GPU die as well as the heatsink. Once it has been cleaned, apply the new thermal paste onto the GPU die (preferably in an X pattern). Make sure to use high-quality thermal paste for this application.

It is also advised to change the thermal pads once you open up the graphics card. The thermal pads sit between the heatsink assembly and the VRM/VRAM components and are responsible for heat dissipation from those dies. To change the thermal pads, you will need to acquire new thermal pads of the same dimensions (especially the height) since any variation in height can lead to improper contact between the GPU die and the heatsink. Once all thermal components are replaced, you can go ahead and close the graphics card again.

Related
Best thermal paste for GPUs
Beat the heat with these excellent thermal paste solutions for your graphics card.

9 Installing a water block

For those who seek maximum cooling performance

For users seeking advanced cooling solutions, installing a water block is an option that can yield extremely impressive results. Water cooling systems circulate a liquid coolant through a block attached to the GPU, providing highly efficient heat dissipation. However, this particular step requires a custom liquid cooling loop to be already present in your PC. Essentially, you can remove the heatsink assembly from the graphics card and attach a custom water block directly to the PCB in order to bring the GPU into your custom loop.

Water cooling setups are capable of maintaining significantly lower temperatures than air cooling solutions, although they require careful installation and maintenance and may come at a higher cost. This method is often preferred by overclockers and enthusiasts seeking maximum cooling performance. It also comes with a risk of damaging the internal components of the graphics card during installation, and even in the event of an unfortunate accident.

Related
Watercooling your PC: What is it and should you do it?
When it comes to cooling computer components, water cooling is the most premium option, but it's not just for high-end systems.

10 Reducing in-game settings

When nothing else works

Cyberpunk 2077 performance benchmark with GeForce Experience "Optimized" settings.

One of the most straightforward ways to lower GPU temperatures is by adjusting in-game graphics settings. Lowering settings such as resolution, texture quality, anti-aliasing, and shadow details can significantly reduce the load on your GPU. This, in turn, leads to lower heat generation during gameplay without compromising overall performance. This is, however, a workaround rather than an actual solution, which is why I consider it a sort of last resort.

Final words

At the end of the day, effectively managing and lowering GPU temperatures is essential for both the longevity and optimal performance of your graphics card. By implementing a combination of the methods discussed, you can ensure your GPU operates within safe temperature ranges, resulting in a stable and reliable computing experience. Remember, a well-cooled GPU not only showcases better performance and extends its lifespan but also enhances your overall gaming enjoyment.

The GPU, or graphics processing unit, is one of the core components of a PC that does a lot of heavy lifting, even when you are not playing a game. It's constantly used in varying capacities by a wide range of applications, and it's important to keep it cool at all times to ensure smooth performance. It's not particularly difficult to tame the temperature of a graphics card, but there are times when you may need to put in some additional efforts to maintain optimal performance. Nobody likes a GPU that's constantly overheating and delivering throttled performance, so read on to find some effective ways to control and lower GPU temperatures, thus safeguarding your graphics card's performance and longevity.

1 Improve case airflow

A simple and effective way to lower temperature

Optimizing the airflow within your computer case is a fundamental step in controlling GPU temperature. Modern GPUs use an array of downdraft fans that force air straight down through the card, dissipating the heat from the heatsink assembly in the process. Adequate case airflow ensures a continuous influx of cool air and efficient removal of hot air, essential for maintaining a balanced temperature.

To enhance case airflow, consider installing additional case fans in strategic locations, configuring fan orientations for optimal air movement, and establishing positive air pressure within the case to prevent dust infiltration and maintain efficient cooling. It is generally agreed upon that positive case pressure provides the ideal balance between thermal performance and dust buildup, although negative pressure can also be quite beneficial for GPU temperatures.

Related
PC airflow guide: How to set up your case fans
You'll need to set up proper airflow inside your PC case for optimal cooling. This guide will help you with that.

2 Increase fan speed

It only takes a few minutes to set a custom fan curve

Modern GPUs come equipped with built-in fans that adjust their speeds automatically based on temperature. However, you can take control of the situation by manually increasing fan speed using software tools like MSI Afterburner or the manufacturer's proprietary software. While this can effectively cool the GPU, be prepared for increased noise levels as a trade-off. Constantly running GPU fans at their maximum speed can also accelerate the wear and tear process.

Related
How to use Fan Control
Want to set up Fan Control to manage your system fans? Here's how.

3 Manage ambient temperature

Try to maintain a low ambient temperature

The ambient temperature in the room where your computer resides plays a pivotal role in GPU temperature management. A warmer room means the GPU has to work harder to dissipate heat, potentially leading to overheating. To mitigate this, ensure proper room ventilation, consider using air conditioning or fans to maintain a cooler room temperature, and avoid placing the computer in a confined space with limited air circulation.

4 Prevent dust buildup

Regular maintenance of your PC is crucial

Dust accumulation on the GPU's heatsinks and fans is a common culprit for elevated temperatures. Although it may seem rather harmless, dust is a menace to electronics, particularly those that work in a confined space. Regular maintenance is the key to preventing dust buildup. Keep your PC dust-free, and make sure that all the case filters are regularly cleaned as well. Use compressed air or specialized electronic cleaning tools to remove dust from your GPU and other components, allowing for unobstructed airflow and efficient cooling.

Related
How to clean the dust out of your PC tower
Dust buildup is bad for your system, so you should regularly clean your PC tower to keep it free of dirt and grime

5 Improve GPU placement

Leave some space for the GPU to breathe

The location of your GPU within the case can significantly impact its temperature. Ideally, install your GPU in the top PCIe slot to ensure it has ample space for airflow and heat dissipation. Moreover, it is also important to consider what orientation you want to install the graphics card in. Although the rising trend of vertical GPU mounting is quite attractive from an aesthetics standpoint, it raises the temperature of the GPU due to reduced airflow. Try to install the GPU in a horizontal position, with plenty of space between the GPU fans and the bottom of the case. Avoid obstructing the GPU with other components, cables, or storage drives, as this can impede the airflow and lead to increased temperatures.

Related
How to install a graphics card in your PC: A beginners guide
There's never a bad time to upgrade and install a new GPU. In this guide, we'll teach you how to install a new graphics card.

6 Improve cable management

Don't slack with cable management

Disorganized and tangled cables can obstruct airflow pathways and disrupt heat dissipation. To neatly organize your cables, you can use cable ties, and if necessary, remove and re-install your CPU cooler with better fans and CPU pump cable routing. Re-route the cables in a manner that ensures a clean and tidy interior, creating an unobstructed channel for airflow. This will ultimately reduce GPU temperatures. Be especially vigilant in ensuring that no cables obstruct the airflow from the intake fans to the GPU fans.

Related
The step-by-step guide for perfect PC cable management
PC cable management is an important part of the PC building process. Here's a step-by-step PC cable management guide to help you get started.

7 GPU undervolting

For those who don't mind tinkering

Undervolting is our first “invasive” step that can significantly improve your GPU temperatures, although with a bit of effort. It involves decreasing the voltage supplied to the GPU while maintaining stability in terms of performance. Undervolting is similar to overclocking, but you are essentially going the opposite way with the voltage slider and trying to achieve stability.

Undervolting can be done using software like MSI Afterburner or with manufacturer-provided tools. Essentially, it involves reducing the voltage provided to the GPU core while maintaining the stock clocks of the graphics card. Each round of undervolting should be followed by thorough stability testing if you don’t want to run into a sudden crash. To get a good measure of the overall stability, it is important to run multiple cycles on various software like Unigine Heaven, Unigine Superposition, 3DMark Suite, Furmark, and your favorite games.

By undervolting your GPU using manufacturer-provided tools or third-party applications, you can significantly reduce heat generation and power consumption. However, it's essential to find the right balance between undervolting and maintaining optimal performance, as excessive undervolting can lead to instability and a reduction in potential boost clocks.

8 Changing thermal paste

Requires a lot of effort

The thermal paste that is applied between the GPU die and its heatsink degrades over time, reducing its heat transfer efficiency. Replacing the old thermal paste with a fresh, high-quality thermal paste can substantially lower temperatures and improve cooling performance. This maintenance task can be particularly beneficial if your GPU is exhibiting signs of overheating, and is often the go-to method for enthusiasts who want to reduce GPU temperatures.

For this step, you will need to open your graphics card to separate the PCB from the heatsink assembly. Most graphics card manufacturers void your warranty for opening up the graphics card, so be mindful of this caveat. Once the two components are separated, thoroughly clean the old thermal paste application from the GPU die as well as the heatsink. Once it has been cleaned, apply the new thermal paste onto the GPU die (preferably in an X pattern). Make sure to use high-quality thermal paste for this application.

It is also advised to change the thermal pads once you open up the graphics card. The thermal pads sit between the heatsink assembly and the VRM/VRAM components and are responsible for heat dissipation from those dies. To change the thermal pads, you will need to acquire new thermal pads of the same dimensions (especially the height) since any variation in height can lead to improper contact between the GPU die and the heatsink. Once all thermal components are replaced, you can go ahead and close the graphics card again.

Related
Best thermal paste for GPUs
Beat the heat with these excellent thermal paste solutions for your graphics card.

9 Installing a water block

For those who seek maximum cooling performance

For users seeking advanced cooling solutions, installing a water block is an option that can yield extremely impressive results. Water cooling systems circulate a liquid coolant through a block attached to the GPU, providing highly efficient heat dissipation. However, this particular step requires a custom liquid cooling loop to be already present in your PC. Essentially, you can remove the heatsink assembly from the graphics card and attach a custom water block directly to the PCB in order to bring the GPU into your custom loop.

Water cooling setups are capable of maintaining significantly lower temperatures than air cooling solutions, although they require careful installation and maintenance and may come at a higher cost. This method is often preferred by overclockers and enthusiasts seeking maximum cooling performance. It also comes with a risk of damaging the internal components of the graphics card during installation, and even in the event of an unfortunate accident.

Related
Watercooling your PC: What is it and should you do it?
When it comes to cooling computer components, water cooling is the most premium option, but it's not just for high-end systems.

10 Reducing in-game settings

When nothing else works

Cyberpunk 2077 performance benchmark with GeForce Experience "Optimized" settings.

One of the most straightforward ways to lower GPU temperatures is by adjusting in-game graphics settings. Lowering settings such as resolution, texture quality, anti-aliasing, and shadow details can significantly reduce the load on your GPU. This, in turn, leads to lower heat generation during gameplay without compromising overall performance. This is, however, a workaround rather than an actual solution, which is why I consider it a sort of last resort.

Final words

At the end of the day, effectively managing and lowering GPU temperatures is essential for both the longevity and optimal performance of your graphics card. By implementing a combination of the methods discussed, you can ensure your GPU operates within safe temperature ranges, resulting in a stable and reliable computing experience. Remember, a well-cooled GPU not only showcases better performance and extends its lifespan but also enhances your overall gaming enjoyment.

item.title: Best PC gaming handhelds in 2024
item.itunes:title: Best PC gaming handhelds in 2024
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
You can now play many of your favorite PC games on the go, thanks to the rise of the PC gaming handheld. They differ from consoles or smartphones, running on the Windows 11 or Linux operating systems, and can access a vast library of PC games from various platforms such as Steam or GoG. While the trend started mostly with the Steam Deck, there are now plenty of excellent alternatives. We weighed the selections based on their performance, design, battery life, and price. Whether you are looking for a powerful machine that can handle the latest AAA titles or a budget-friendly option for playing the newest indie games, there is something for you.

  • Source: Valve
    Steam Deck OLED
    Best overall
    $549 at Steam
  • Steam Deck
    Best value
    $399 at Steam
  • Source: Ayaneo
    Ayaneo KUN
    Premium pick
    $999 at IndieGoGo
  • Source: GPD
    GPD Win 4
    Best convertible
    $700 at IndieGoGo
  • ASUS ROG Ally
    Best Windows handheld
    $400 at Best Buy (Ryzen Z1)
  • Lenovo Legion Go
    Switch-like
    $700 at Best Buy
  • Source: AyaNeo
    Ayaneo Slide
    Best compact pick
    See at IndieGoGo

Our picks for the best PC gaming handhelds in 2023

Steam Deck OLED
Best overall

An even better Steam Deck

Valve recently announced an updated Steam Deck with an updated OLED edition with a larger display and support for HDR. Valve has also increased the battery capacity, upgraded the Wi-Fi, and increased the maximum storage capacity up to 1TB. If you're looking for the best version of the best PC gaming handheld, the Steam Deck OLED is for you.

Pros
  • Improved battery life
  • OLED display with HDR support
  • New 1TB max storage capacity
Cons
  • Bulky and large
  • Graphics performance remains unchanged
$549 at Steam

Valve's Steam Deck was our favorite PC gaming handheld of 2022 and was one of the first PC gaming handhelds to truly revolutionize the PC gaming industry. In 2024, however, Valve faces fierce competition from various companies that have begun making their own handhelds. Valve is keeping up with the competition, though, and recently released an even better version of the original Steam Deck called the Steam Deck OLED.

The Steam Deck OLED, as the name implies, features an upgraded OLED display with HDR support alongside upgraded Wi-Fi 6E, slightly faster LPDDR5 RAM, and it now supports up to 1TB of storage space. Valve didn't change too much else under the hood, opting to keep the same 6nm AMD Zen 2-based processor with a base clock speed of 2.4GHz that can be overclocked to 3.5GHz. While you could play most of your Steam games in the original version thanks in part to the Linux-based SteamOS, they'll now look better and run smoother for longer.

If you had been waiting for a new Steam Deck to come out before committing to a purchase, there is no greater time to pick one up. It's not a reinvention of the Steam Deck, which was an already great PC gaming handheld, but it offers improvements in the areas where the Steam Deck needed it most, particularly the display and wireless standard speeds. And with those changes, the Steam Deck OLED takes our top spot.

Steam Deck
Best value

The original Steam Deck is still worth a look

If the $550 starting price of the Steam Deck OLED feels a bit too high for you, Valve's original Steam Deck is still available and is now the best entry-level PC gaming handheld. It has enough power to play most of the games in your Steam library, and it's now more affordable, starting at $400.

Pros
  • Now cheaper than ever
  • Significant user control
  • Powerful hardware
Cons
  • Lackluster display
  • Limited battery life
$399 at Steam

If you are interested in handheld gaming but don't want to spend $550 on the base configuration of the Steam Deck OLED, you can still pick up the original Steam Deck for a starting price of $399. The original Steam Deck features many of the same specs as the upgraded Steam Deck OLED, but it comes at a more affordable price and doesn't pack the same kind of punch for the display.

The Steam Deck has a 7-inch LCD panel, up to 512GB of NVMe storage space, and the same AMD Zen 2 APU CPU found in the Steam Deck OLED. Performance-wise, the Steam Deck excels at handling most games from your library. In our review, our colleague Adam Conway could play games such as Grand Theft Auto V and Metro 2033: Remastered at 60FPS, but he did note that battery life suffered in AAA at 60FPS.

Where the original Steam Deck really shines now that the Steam Deck OLED has been released is as a device for emulation or to play the large selection of indie games from the Steam Library. The battery life won't be as good as on the OLED and visuals won't look as vivid, but otherwise, you'll get the same great system, with SteamOS and Proton running the show.

Ayaneo KUN
Premium pick

The most premium handheld experience

$999 $1209 Save $210

The Ayaneo Kun is a premium PC gaming handheld with a powerful AMD Ryzen 7 7840U processor, a bright, high-resolution 8.4-inch display, and a large battery. It's designed for gamers who want to experience handheld gaming at the highest level.

Pros
  • Powerful AMD processor
  • Bright 8.4-inch display
  • Large 75Wh battery
Cons
  • Windows 11 OS struggles at times on handheld systems
  • Very expensive
  • Can get hot under heavy loads
$999 at IndieGoGo

The Ayaneo Kun is a premium Windows gaming handheld that aims to compete with the Steam Deck and other PC gaming handhelds. And it can, in a lot of ways, thanks to the powerful AMD Ryzen 7 7840U processor with integrated Radeon 700M graphics, which makes it capable of running most modern games with decent performance settings. It also has a large 8.4-inch IPS LCD screen with a native 1600p resolution, offering great color performance and a peak brightness of 500 nits.

Performance-wise, the Ayaneo Kun delivers an excellent gaming experience. There's a wide range of configuration options thanks to Ayaneo's AYA Space controls within Windows 11, allowing you to set different power draws and graphics settings to get the most out of your handheld PC gaming experience. We noted in our review that the Ayaneo Kun becomes a fantastic emulation device when using a lower TDP, but at higher power draws, battery life suffers greatly.

Ultimately, the Ayaneo Kun is one of the most powerful PC gaming handhelds on the market, but it comes at a premium starting price of around $1,200. Our biggest complaint with the Ayaneo Kun is related to the issues of Windows 11 performance on gaming handhelds, but Ayaneo has even improved this overall experience by providing users with the AYA Space tool. While it is a great device, it is extremely expensive, making it our top premium pick.

GPD Win 4
Best convertible

Gaming and business in one handheld

The GPD Win 4 is a handheld gaming PC that combines portability, performance, and versatility. It features a 6.5-inch Full HD touchscreen, a backlit keyboard, and a built-in gamepad with dual analog sticks alongside a Thunderbolt 4 charging port. The GPD Win 4 ships with Windows 11 and is capable of running your favorite AAA games and emulations. 

Pros
  • Powerful performance
  • Large battery
  • Full keyboard and mouse support
Cons
  • Hot temperatures when under stress
$700 at IndieGoGo$864 at Amazon

The GPD Win 4 is a PC gaming handheld that combines portability, performance, and versatility. The handheld has a 6-inch IPS display with ultra-narrow bezels, providing a native resolution of 1920x1080, and there's support for a 10-point touch and active stylus, although you have to buy the pen separately. The display features a maximum brightness of 400 nits, making it one of the brighter displays on the handheld market.

The most unique feature of the GPD Win Max 2 is the full keyboard that sits underneath the display, in addition to the dual analog sticks, mappable back buttons, and analog triggers. It's easy to switch between the keyboard and controller, and there's even a dedicated button that switches between gamepad and keyboard support. Design-wise, the GPD Win 4 looks nearly identical to Sony's PS Vita.

Otherwise, the GPD Win 4 features up to an AMD Ryzen 7 7840U APU processor with AMD Radeon 760M integrated graphics, up to 64GB of LPDDR5x RAM, and up to 4TB of PCIe 4.0 SSD storage, which you can expand thanks to an easily accessible M.2 2280 slot on the underside of the machine. There's even support for an external GPU via the Max 2's Oculink port or the USB4 port for even more gaming power.

The GPD Win 4 is a powerful, versatile handheld that can handle AAA games, emulators, productivity apps, and more. It's the ultimate device for gamers who want to enjoy their favorite games on the go while also having the option to get some business done without needing a large laptop. It's not a laptop, but it does feature an optional 4G LTE module, a full-sized PCIe, and is one of the most powerful slide-out handhelds on the market.

ASUS ROG Ally
Best Windows handheld

Windows 11 gaming on a handheld

$400 $600 Save $200

The Asus ROG Ally is a handheld gaming device that runs on Windows 11 and allows you to access your game libraries from various platforms. A 7-inch touchscreen with a 120Hz refresh rate delivers immersive visuals, and an AMD Ryzen Z1 Extreme APU offers high performance and low power consumption for a better battery life.

Pros
  • More powerful internals than Steam Deck
  • Upgrading storage is easy
  • Adjustable power draw
Cons
  • Issues with OS updates
  • Quality control issues
  • Windows 11 isn't the best for touchscreens
$400 at Best Buy (Ryzen Z1)$600 at Best Buy (Ryzen Z1 Extreme)$700 at Asus

The Asus ROG Ally is a portable handheld that differs from the Steam Deck because it runs on Windows 11 and, as such, features support for Steam, Xbox Game Pass, Epic, and just about every other game library. The ROG Ally features a 7-inch touchscreen with a 120Hz refresh rate, 465 nits of peak brightness, and AMD FreeSync Premium support to deliver smooth visuals and gameplay. Additionally, the ROG Ally is powered by a Ryzen Z1 or Z1 Extreme processor, 16GB of LPDDR5 RAM, and a 512GB PCIe4 SSD to give you the ultimate portable gaming experience.

The ROG Ally is designed to be used from anywhere, whether you want to play solo or with friends. It features a lightweight and durable body that weighs only 608 grams, making it lighter than the Steam Deck. The controls are customizable and allow you to create your own control mapping, while there's also support for Asus' Aura Sync lighting system and ROG Armoury Crate SE software for game management.

The largest downside to the ROG Ally is how Windows 11 functions on the device. In our review of the ROG Ally, we noted that Windows doesn't work well on touchscreen devices like the ROG Ally. However, ASUS has updated the BIOS towards the end of 2023. While many of these issues have been corrected, Windows 11 still doesn't offer the best experience in handheld gaming.

Lenovo Legion Go
Switch-like

A switch-like portable gaming machine

The Lenovo Legion Go features an interesting Nintendo Switch-like design, thanks to its detachable controllers, but, it features a gorgeous 8.8-inch display with a 16:10 aspect ratio. One of the detachable controllers features a trackpad that can be used a mouse, and there's external keyboard support in addition to the built-in gamepad.

Pros
  • 2.5K Resolution display
  • Detachable controllers
  • Solid battery life
Cons
  • Heavy design at 820g
  • Limited storage options
$700 at Best Buy$700 at Lenovo$700 at B&H

The Lenovo Legion Go is a handheld gaming PC with up to an AMD Ryzen Z1 Extreme processor with 8 cores and 16 threads of processing power alongside AMD RDNA 3 Graphics for high-performance gaming. The Legion Go from Lenovo will be comfortable for anyone who has gamed on a Nintendo Switch before, thanks to its detachable controllers.

Thanks to the AMD Z1 Extreme processor, the Legion Go utilizes AMD RDNA graphics, providing excellent, smooth visuals and high framerates even in AAA titles. There's also support for DirectX 12 Ultimate, Vulkan, and OpenGL 4.6, so this is a handheld that can handle various games and applications with ease. This is a handheld that is able to compete with the Steam Deck thanks to a more powerful processor and GPU. Games also look great thanks to the 16:10 aspect ratio complementing the 8.8-inch display.

It's 8.8-inch display looms large over other portable handhelds such as the Steam Deck and ASUS Rog Ally, and it offers one of the fastest screens on the market, with a 144Hz refresh rate, compared to the 120Hz refresh rate found on the Steam Deck OLED. It's by no means a perfect device, with performance issues noted in our review. However, Lenovo has been steadily making software-based improvements to the Legion Go, and with a great suite of hardware, it's still a great purchase for anyone looking for a fast, responsive, and high-resolution screen.

Ayaneo Slide
Best compact pick

A more portable experience with great visuals

The Ayaneo Slide is gaming handheld with a slidable screen, revealing a backlit keyboard underneath. It features an AMD Ryzen 7 7840U, up to 64GB of LPDDR5x RAM, and up to 4TB of storage space.

Pros
  • Sliding keyboard
  • Ergonomic design
  • Powerful performance
Cons
  • Sliding mechanism feels cheap
  • Poor battery life
See at IndieGoGo

The Ayaneo Slide is another premium handheld gaming system that runs on Windows 11 and can play your favorite PC games on a 6-inch 1080p Full HD display. It's powered by an AMD Ryzen 7 7840U processor with integrated AMD Radeon graphics and starts with 16GB of RAM and a 512GB PCIe 4.0 NVMe SSD for storage space. It comes with a standard controller layout on the face of the device featuring two joysticks, a d-pad, and four d-pad buttons, in addition to haptic triggers on the back. In our review of the Ayaneo Slide, our Lead Technical Editor, Adam Conway, noted that while the Ayaneo Slide feels like a well-built device, the sliding mechanism does feel a bit cheap overall.

When you slide open the Slide, there's a full QWERTY keyboard. The design is reminiscent of the T-Mobile Sidekick cellphone from the early 2000's, but with a full controller on the sides. The ultimate feature of the Ayaneo Slide is how many configurations of the device you can purchase. Ayaneo always offers a lot of configurations for their portables, and the Slide is no different, with a top configuration that offers 64GB of RAM and a 2TB SSD.

The Ayaneo Slide is designed to offer a smooth gaming experience for AAA titles and emulators, and is nearly identical in performance when compared to the ASUS ROG Ally and Lenovo Legion Go. In our review, we noted that the Ayaneo Slide offered an exceptional experience when playing Call of Duty: Modern Warfare II.

GPD Win Mini
Best with keyboard

A more compact GPD Win

The GPD Win Mini is a handheld gaming device that combines the power of a Windows 11 PC with the portability of a smartphone. It features a 6-inch touchscreen in a fold-out design with a full QWERTY keyboard, and a built-in gamepad. 

Pros
  • Compact and portable design
  • Support for various gaming platforms
  • Fold out QWERTY Keyboard
Cons
  • Small screen size
  • Expensive

The GPD Win Mini is a handheld gaming PC that combines the power of a laptop with the portability of a smartphone. This is a truly compact handheld that features a 7-inch display that supports a 10-point touch and a native resolution of 1920x1080 with up to a 120 Hz refresh rate. Underneath the display, though, is the highlight feature: a full QWERTY keyboard that folds out, and the face of the device features a built-in gamepad with analog sticks and buttons.

The GPD Win Mini runs on Windows 11 and can play most PC games at high settings thanks to the AMD Ryzen 7 7840U processor and integrated Radeon 780M graphics. It ships with up to 32GB of LPDDR5 RAM, making it an efficient device when it comes to gaming, and you can purchase the GPD Win Mini with up to 2TB of SSD storage space.

The GPD Win Mini is designed for gamers who want to enjoy their favorite PC titles on the go. Whether you're playing games from Steam, the Epic Games Store, or GOG, GPD Win 3 can do it all thanks to Windows 11 support.

Best PC gaming handhelds: What you need to know

The Steam Deck OLED is one of our favorite handhelds, and for most people, it's the best handheld to buy. It features a beautiful 7-inch OLED display, a custom AMD APU, and a full-fledged Linux-based operating system called SteamOS, which was developed by Valve and optimized for the Steam Deck. It offers a portable and versatile gaming experience and has the ability to play thousands of titles from Steam's library, as well as other platforms and services. There's also a wide range of accessories available for the Steam Deck.

Steam Deck OLED
Editor's Choice

The best handheld for most people

Valve's upgraded Steam Deck features a larger OLED display with HDR support, faster Wi-Fi, and a bigger battery. Plus, this new model is slightly lighter, has slightly faster RAM, and it comes with storage up to 1TB. If you're looking for the ultimate Steam Deck, this is the version for you.

$549 at Steam

There's a wide range of configuration options thanks to Ayaneo's AYA Space controls within Windows 11, allowing you to set different power draws and graphics settings to get the most out of your handheld PC gaming experience. We noted in our review that the Ayaneo Kun becomes a fantastic emulation device when using a lower TDP, but at higher power draws, battery life suffers greatly.

Ultimately, the Ayaneo Kun is one of the most powerful PC gaming handhelds on the market, but it comes at a premium starting price of around $1,200. Our biggest complaint with the Ayaneo Kun is related to the issues of Windows 11 performance on gaming handhelds, but Ayaneo has even improved this overall experience by providing users with the AYA Space tool. While it is a great device, it is extremely expensive, making it our top premium pick.

GPD Win 4
Best convertible

Gaming and business in one handheld

The GPD Win 4 is a handheld gaming PC that combines portability, performance, and versatility. It features a 6.5-inch Full HD touchscreen, a backlit keyboard, and a built-in gamepad with dual analog sticks alongside a Thunderbolt 4 charging port. The GPD Win 4 ships with Windows 11 and is capable of running your favorite AAA games and emulations. 

Pros
  • Powerful performance
  • Large battery
  • Full keyboard and mouse support
Cons
  • Hot temperatures when under stress
$700 at IndieGoGo$864 at Amazon

The GPD Win 4 is a PC gaming handheld that combines portability, performance, and versatility. The handheld has a 6-inch IPS display with ultra-narrow bezels, providing a native resolution of 1920x1080, and there's support for a 10-point touch and active stylus, although you have to buy the pen separately. The display features a maximum brightness of 400 nits, making it one of the brighter displays on the handheld market.

The most unique feature of the GPD Win Max 2 is the full keyboard that sits underneath the display, in addition to the dual analog sticks, mappable back buttons, and analog triggers. It's easy to switch between the keyboard and controller, and there's even a dedicated button that switches between gamepad and keyboard support. Design-wise, the GPD Win 4 looks nearly identical to Sony's PS Vita.

Otherwise, the GPD Win 4 features up to an AMD Ryzen 7 7840U APU processor with AMD Radeon 760M integrated graphics, up to 64GB of LPDDR5x RAM, and up to 4TB of PCIe 4.0 SSD storage, which you can expand thanks to an easily accessible M.2 2280 slot on the underside of the machine. There's even support for an external GPU via the Max 2's Oculink port or the USB4 port for even more gaming power.

The GPD Win 4 is a powerful, versatile handheld that can handle AAA games, emulators, productivity apps, and more. It's the ultimate device for gamers who want to enjoy their favorite games on the go while also having the option to get some business done without needing a large laptop. It's not a laptop, but it does feature an optional 4G LTE module, a full-sized PCIe, and is one of the most powerful slide-out handhelds on the market.

ASUS ROG Ally
Best Windows handheld

Windows 11 gaming on a handheld

$400 $600 Save $200

The Asus ROG Ally is a handheld gaming device that runs on Windows 11 and allows you to access your game libraries from various platforms. A 7-inch touchscreen with a 120Hz refresh rate delivers immersive visuals, and an AMD Ryzen Z1 Extreme APU offers high performance and low power consumption for a better battery life.

Pros
  • More powerful internals than Steam Deck
  • Upgrading storage is easy
  • Adjustable power draw
Cons
  • Issues with OS updates
  • Quality control issues
  • Windows 11 isn't the best for touchscreens
$400 at Best Buy (Ryzen Z1)$600 at Best Buy (Ryzen Z1 Extreme)$700 at Asus

The Asus ROG Ally is a portable handheld that differs from the Steam Deck because it runs on Windows 11 and, as such, features support for Steam, Xbox Game Pass, Epic, and just about every other game library. The ROG Ally features a 7-inch touchscreen with a 120Hz refresh rate, 465 nits of peak brightness, and AMD FreeSync Premium support to deliver smooth visuals and gameplay. Additionally, the ROG Ally is powered by a Ryzen Z1 or Z1 Extreme processor, 16GB of LPDDR5 RAM, and a 512GB PCIe4 SSD to give you the ultimate portable gaming experience.

The ROG Ally is designed to be used from anywhere, whether you want to play solo or with friends. It features a lightweight and durable body that weighs only 608 grams, making it lighter than the Steam Deck. The controls are customizable and allow you to create your own control mapping, while there's also support for Asus' Aura Sync lighting system and ROG Armoury Crate SE software for game management.

The largest downside to the ROG Ally is how Windows 11 functions on the device. In our review of the ROG Ally, we noted that Windows doesn't work well on touchscreen devices like the ROG Ally. However, ASUS has updated the BIOS towards the end of 2023. While many of these issues have been corrected, Windows 11 still doesn't offer the best experience in handheld gaming.

Lenovo Legion Go
Switch-like

A switch-like portable gaming machine

The Lenovo Legion Go features an interesting Nintendo Switch-like design, thanks to its detachable controllers, but, it features a gorgeous 8.8-inch display with a 16:10 aspect ratio. One of the detachable controllers features a trackpad that can be used a mouse, and there's external keyboard support in addition to the built-in gamepad.

Pros
  • 2.5K Resolution display
  • Detachable controllers
  • Solid battery life
Cons
  • Heavy design at 820g
  • Limited storage options
$700 at Best Buy$700 at Lenovo$700 at B&H

The Lenovo Legion Go is a handheld gaming PC with up to an AMD Ryzen Z1 Extreme processor with 8 cores and 16 threads of processing power alongside AMD RDNA 3 Graphics for high-performance gaming. The Legion Go from Lenovo will be comfortable for anyone who has gamed on a Nintendo Switch before, thanks to its detachable controllers.

Thanks to the AMD Z1 Extreme processor, the Legion Go utilizes AMD RDNA graphics, providing excellent, smooth visuals and high framerates even in AAA titles. There's also support for DirectX 12 Ultimate, Vulkan, and OpenGL 4.6, so this is a handheld that can handle various games and applications with ease. This is a handheld that is able to compete with the Steam Deck thanks to a more powerful processor and GPU. Games also look great thanks to the 16:10 aspect ratio complementing the 8.8-inch display.

It's 8.8-inch display looms large over other portable handhelds such as the Steam Deck and ASUS Rog Ally, and it offers one of the fastest screens on the market, with a 144Hz refresh rate, compared to the 120Hz refresh rate found on the Steam Deck OLED. It's by no means a perfect device, with performance issues noted in our review. However, Lenovo has been steadily making software-based improvements to the Legion Go, and with a great suite of hardware, it's still a great purchase for anyone looking for a fast, responsive, and high-resolution screen.

Ayaneo Slide
Best compact pick

A more portable experience with great visuals

The Ayaneo Slide is gaming handheld with a slidable screen, revealing a backlit keyboard underneath. It features an AMD Ryzen 7 7840U, up to 64GB of LPDDR5x RAM, and up to 4TB of storage space.

Pros
  • Sliding keyboard
  • Ergonomic design
  • Powerful performance
Cons
  • Sliding mechanism feels cheap
  • Poor battery life
See at IndieGoGo

The Ayaneo Slide is another premium handheld gaming system that runs on Windows 11 and can play your favorite PC games on a 6-inch 1080p Full HD display. It's powered by an AMD Ryzen 7 7840U processor with integrated AMD Radeon graphics and starts with 16GB of RAM and a 512GB PCIe 4.0 NVMe SSD for storage space. It comes with a standard controller layout on the face of the device featuring two joysticks, a d-pad, and four d-pad buttons, in addition to haptic triggers on the back. In our review of the Ayaneo Slide, our Lead Technical Editor, Adam Conway, noted that while the Ayaneo Slide feels like a well-built device, the sliding mechanism does feel a bit cheap overall.

When you slide open the Slide, there's a full QWERTY keyboard. The design is reminiscent of the T-Mobile Sidekick cellphone from the early 2000's, but with a full controller on the sides. The ultimate feature of the Ayaneo Slide is how many configurations of the device you can purchase. Ayaneo always offers a lot of configurations for their portables, and the Slide is no different, with a top configuration that offers 64GB of RAM and a 2TB SSD.

The Ayaneo Slide is designed to offer a smooth gaming experience for AAA titles and emulators, and is nearly identical in performance when compared to the ASUS ROG Ally and Lenovo Legion Go. In our review, we noted that the Ayaneo Slide offered an exceptional experience when playing Call of Duty: Modern Warfare II.

GPD Win Mini
Best with keyboard

A more compact GPD Win

The GPD Win Mini is a handheld gaming device that combines the power of a Windows 11 PC with the portability of a smartphone. It features a 6-inch touchscreen in a fold-out design with a full QWERTY keyboard, and a built-in gamepad. 

Pros
  • Compact and portable design
  • Support for various gaming platforms
  • Fold out QWERTY Keyboard
Cons
  • Small screen size
  • Expensive

The GPD Win Mini is a handheld gaming PC that combines the power of a laptop with the portability of a smartphone. This is a truly compact handheld that features a 7-inch display that supports a 10-point touch and a native resolution of 1920x1080 with up to a 120 Hz refresh rate. Underneath the display, though, is the highlight feature: a full QWERTY keyboard that folds out, and the face of the device features a built-in gamepad with analog sticks and buttons.

The GPD Win Mini runs on Windows 11 and can play most PC games at high settings thanks to the AMD Ryzen 7 7840U processor and integrated Radeon 780M graphics. It ships with up to 32GB of LPDDR5 RAM, making it an efficient device when it comes to gaming, and you can purchase the GPD Win Mini with up to 2TB of SSD storage space.

The GPD Win Mini is designed for gamers who want to enjoy their favorite PC titles on the go. Whether you're playing games from Steam, the Epic Games Store, or GOG, GPD Win 3 can do it all thanks to Windows 11 support.

Best PC gaming handhelds: What you need to know

The Steam Deck OLED is one of our favorite handhelds, and for most people, it's the best handheld to buy. It features a beautiful 7-inch OLED display, a custom AMD APU, and a full-fledged Linux-based operating system called SteamOS, which was developed by Valve and optimized for the Steam Deck. It offers a portable and versatile gaming experience and has the ability to play thousands of titles from Steam's library, as well as other platforms and services. There's also a wide range of accessories available for the Steam Deck.

Steam Deck OLED
Editor's Choice

The best handheld for most people

Valve's upgraded Steam Deck features a larger OLED display with HDR support, faster Wi-Fi, and a bigger battery. Plus, this new model is slightly lighter, has slightly faster RAM, and it comes with storage up to 1TB. If you're looking for the ultimate Steam Deck, this is the version for you.

$549 at Steam
item.content:encoded:
You can now play many of your favorite PC games on the go, thanks to the rise of the PC gaming handheld. They differ from consoles or smartphones, running on the Windows 11 or Linux operating systems, and can access a vast library of PC games from various platforms such as Steam or GoG. While the trend started mostly with the Steam Deck, there are now plenty of excellent alternatives. We weighed the selections based on their performance, design, battery life, and price. Whether you are looking for a powerful machine that can handle the latest AAA titles or a budget-friendly option for playing the newest indie games, there is something for you.

  • Source: Valve
    Steam Deck OLED
    Best overall
    $549 at Steam
  • Steam Deck
    Best value
    $399 at Steam
  • Source: Ayaneo
    Ayaneo KUN
    Premium pick
    $999 at IndieGoGo
  • Source: GPD
    GPD Win 4
    Best convertible
    $700 at IndieGoGo
  • ASUS ROG Ally
    Best Windows handheld
    $400 at Best Buy (Ryzen Z1)
  • Lenovo Legion Go
    Switch-like
    $700 at Best Buy
  • Source: AyaNeo
    Ayaneo Slide
    Best compact pick
    See at IndieGoGo

Our picks for the best PC gaming handhelds in 2023

Steam Deck OLED
Best overall

An even better Steam Deck

Valve recently announced an updated Steam Deck with an updated OLED edition with a larger display and support for HDR. Valve has also increased the battery capacity, upgraded the Wi-Fi, and increased the maximum storage capacity up to 1TB. If you're looking for the best version of the best PC gaming handheld, the Steam Deck OLED is for you.

Pros
  • Improved battery life
  • OLED display with HDR support
  • New 1TB max storage capacity
Cons
  • Bulky and large
  • Graphics performance remains unchanged
$549 at Steam

Valve's Steam Deck was our favorite PC gaming handheld of 2022 and was one of the first PC gaming handhelds to truly revolutionize the PC gaming industry. In 2024, however, Valve faces fierce competition from various companies that have begun making their own handhelds. Valve is keeping up with the competition, though, and recently released an even better version of the original Steam Deck called the Steam Deck OLED.

The Steam Deck OLED, as the name implies, features an upgraded OLED display with HDR support alongside upgraded Wi-Fi 6E, slightly faster LPDDR5 RAM, and it now supports up to 1TB of storage space. Valve didn't change too much else under the hood, opting to keep the same 6nm AMD Zen 2-based processor with a base clock speed of 2.4GHz that can be overclocked to 3.5GHz. While you could play most of your Steam games in the original version thanks in part to the Linux-based SteamOS, they'll now look better and run smoother for longer.

If you had been waiting for a new Steam Deck to come out before committing to a purchase, there is no greater time to pick one up. It's not a reinvention of the Steam Deck, which was an already great PC gaming handheld, but it offers improvements in the areas where the Steam Deck needed it most, particularly the display and wireless standard speeds. And with those changes, the Steam Deck OLED takes our top spot.

Steam Deck
Best value

The original Steam Deck is still worth a look

If the $550 starting price of the Steam Deck OLED feels a bit too high for you, Valve's original Steam Deck is still available and is now the best entry-level PC gaming handheld. It has enough power to play most of the games in your Steam library, and it's now more affordable, starting at $400.

Pros
  • Now cheaper than ever
  • Significant user control
  • Powerful hardware
Cons
  • Lackluster display
  • Limited battery life
$399 at Steam

If you are interested in handheld gaming but don't want to spend $550 on the base configuration of the Steam Deck OLED, you can still pick up the original Steam Deck for a starting price of $399. The original Steam Deck features many of the same specs as the upgraded Steam Deck OLED, but it comes at a more affordable price and doesn't pack the same kind of punch for the display.

The Steam Deck has a 7-inch LCD panel, up to 512GB of NVMe storage space, and the same AMD Zen 2 APU CPU found in the Steam Deck OLED. Performance-wise, the Steam Deck excels at handling most games from your library. In our review, our colleague Adam Conway could play games such as Grand Theft Auto V and Metro 2033: Remastered at 60FPS, but he did note that battery life suffered in AAA at 60FPS.

Where the original Steam Deck really shines now that the Steam Deck OLED has been released is as a device for emulation or to play the large selection of indie games from the Steam Library. The battery life won't be as good as on the OLED and visuals won't look as vivid, but otherwise, you'll get the same great system, with SteamOS and Proton running the show.

Ayaneo KUN
Premium pick

The most premium handheld experience

$999 $1209 Save $210

The Ayaneo Kun is a premium PC gaming handheld with a powerful AMD Ryzen 7 7840U processor, a bright, high-resolution 8.4-inch display, and a large battery. It's designed for gamers who want to experience handheld gaming at the highest level.

Pros
  • Powerful AMD processor
  • Bright 8.4-inch display
  • Large 75Wh battery
Cons
  • Windows 11 OS struggles at times on handheld systems
  • Very expensive
  • Can get hot under heavy loads
$999 at IndieGoGo

The Ayaneo Kun is a premium Windows gaming handheld that aims to compete with the Steam Deck and other PC gaming handhelds. And it can, in a lot of ways, thanks to the powerful AMD Ryzen 7 7840U processor with integrated Radeon 700M graphics, which makes it capable of running most modern games with decent performance settings. It also has a large 8.4-inch IPS LCD screen with a native 1600p resolution, offering great color performance and a peak brightness of 500 nits.

Performance-wise, the Ayaneo Kun delivers an excellent gaming experience. There's a wide range of configuration options thanks to Ayaneo's AYA Space controls within Windows 11, allowing you to set different power draws and graphics settings to get the most out of your handheld PC gaming experience. We noted in our review that the Ayaneo Kun becomes a fantastic emulation device when using a lower TDP, but at higher power draws, battery life suffers greatly.

Ultimately, the Ayaneo Kun is one of the most powerful PC gaming handhelds on the market, but it comes at a premium starting price of around $1,200. Our biggest complaint with the Ayaneo Kun is related to the issues of Windows 11 performance on gaming handhelds, but Ayaneo has even improved this overall experience by providing users with the AYA Space tool. While it is a great device, it is extremely expensive, making it our top premium pick.

The GPD Win 4 ships with Windows 11 and is capable of running your favorite AAA games and emulations.

item.title: Best iPhone 15 Pro Max cases in 2024

item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
The iPhone 15 Pro Max is Apple's best iPhone yet. Packing a ton of advanced technology and support for the latest OS, it's a future-proof device that should last users for many years. Though, its internals don't strictly dictate its life expectancy, as the exterior also plays a role. That's why you should buy a screen protector for your iPhone 15 Pro Max, along with a case. These accessories will ensure that your iPhone's chassis doesn't absorb all of the damage if an accident occurs. To help you find a suitable iPhone 15 Pro Max case, we've listed some of the best options below.
